KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** *Define a digital signal. How does it differ from a continuous-time analog signal in terms of time and amplitude?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** A digital signal is a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude signal represented by a finite set of amplitude levels, most commonly two levels corresponding to logic 1 and logic 0. - **Time domain (1 mark):** An analog signal is defined for all instants of time $t \in \mathbb{R}$, whereas a digital signal is defined only at discrete instants $t = kT$, $k \in \mathbb{Z}$. - **Amplitude domain (1 mark):** An analog signal can take any value in a continuous range, whereas a digital signal is constrained to one of $2^N$ discrete quantization levels determined by the ADC word length $N$. --- **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** *State the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem. What happens if the sampling frequency is less than the Nyquist rate?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Statement (1.5 marks):** A bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \ge 2 f_m$. The minimum rate $f_N = 2 f_m$ is the Nyquist rate. - **Consequence of violation (1.5 marks):** If $f_s < 2 f_m$, **aliasing** occurs. High-frequency components of the original signal fold back into the baseband and appear as false low-frequency components in the reconstructed signal, causing irreversible information loss. An anti-aliasing low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \le f_s/2$ is therefore placed before the sampler in practice. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice]** **(a)** Explain the three stages of analog-to-digital conversion with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each stage in a digital control system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 10-bit unipolar ADC has a full-scale range of 0 to 5 V. Compute: - (i) the resolution (step size) in mV, - (ii) the maximum quantization error, - (iii) the SQNR in dB for a full-scale sinusoidal input. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** The three stages of ADC, in order, are: **1. Sampling (Time Discretization) — 2 marks** A Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit captures the instantaneous amplitude of the continuous signal $x(t)$ at uniform intervals $T$. The output is a Pulse-Amplitude Modulated (PAM) signal $x(kT)$. The sampling period must satisfy $T \le 1/(2 f_m)$ per the Nyquist theorem. In a digital control loop, sampling synchronizes the analog plant to the discrete computational cycle of the controller. **2. Quantization (Amplitude Discretization) — 2.5 marks** The sampled amplitude is rounded to the nearest of $L = 2^N$ discrete levels. For $N=10$, there are 1024 levels, and the resolution is $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$. The rounding operation introduces an irreversible quantization error $e_q \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$. This error sets the noise floor of the digital controller and directly limits achievable control precision. **3. Coding (Binary Encoding) — 2.5 marks** Each quantized level is mapped to a unique $N$-bit binary codeword (Natural Binary, Gray, or Two's Complement for signed values). The output is a serial or parallel bitstream that enters the controller's data bus. The choice of code (e.g., Gray for encoders to avoid transition errors) is critical for signal integrity in industrial environments. **Block Diagram (text representation — students should draw the actual block diagram in the exam):** Sensor → Sample-and-Hold → Quantizer → Encoder → Digital Controller --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Given:** $N = 10$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V (unipolar, range 0–5 V) **Part (i) Resolution (Step Size):** — 2 marks $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.8828 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} \approx 4.88\ \text{mV}$$ **Part (ii) Maximum Quantization Error:** — 2 marks $$\vert e_{q,\max} \vert = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{4.88}{2} = 2.44\ \text{mV}$$ **Part (iii) SQNR in dB:** — 3 marks Using the standard formula for a full-scale sinusoid: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 60.20 + 1.76 = 61.96\ \text{dB} \approx 62\ \text{dB}$$ --- #### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice]** **(a)** With the help of neat timing diagrams, explain any **three** line coding schemes used to represent digital signals. Compare them in terms of bandwidth requirement and DC component. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A temperature sensor outputs an analog signal with maximum frequency component $f_m = 250$ Hz. The digital controller samples this signal using a 12-bit ADC with a full-scale range of 0 to 10 V. - (i) Determine the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. - (ii) Compute the resolution and the maximum quantization error. - (iii) Calculate the SQNR in dB. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Three line coding schemes explained:** **1. Unipolar NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) — 2 marks** - Binary 1 is represented by a constant +V level for the entire bit duration. - Binary 0 is represented by 0 V. - The signal does not return to zero between consecutive 1s. - **Disadvantage:** Strong DC component; long strings of 1s produce no transitions (no self-clocking). **2. Manchester (Biphase-L) — 2.5 marks** - Binary 1 is represented by a **High-to-Low** transition at the middle of the bit period. - Binary 0 is represented by a **Low-to-High** transition at the middle of the bit period. - **Advantages:** No DC component (equal time at +V and −V); guaranteed mid-bit transition provides self-clocking. - **Disadvantage:** Doubled bandwidth compared to NRZ. - **Used in:** 10-Mbps Ethernet (10Base-T), CAN bus in automobiles. **3. AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion) — 2.5 marks** - Binary 1 alternates between +V and −V for successive 1s. - Binary 0 is represented by 0 V. - **Advantages:** No DC component; easy error detection (a violation of alternation indicates a bit error). - **Used in:** T1 digital telephone trunks (1.544 Mbps), ISDN. **Comparison Table (to be drawn in the exam):** | Property | Unipolar NRZ | Manchester | AMI | |---|---|---|---| | Bandwidth | $R_b/2$ | $R_b$ | $\sim R_b/2$ | | DC Component | High | Zero | Zero | | Self-Clocking | No | Yes | Limited | | Error Detection | No | No | Yes | --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Given:** $f_m = 250$ Hz, $N = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V **Part (i) Minimum Sampling Rate:** — 2 marks By the Nyquist criterion: $$f_{s,\min} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 250 = 500\ \text{Hz}$$ **Part (ii) Resolution and Maximum Quantization Error:** — 2 marks Resolution (step size): $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.4414 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} \approx 2.44\ \text{mV}$$ Maximum quantization error: $$\vert e_{q,\max} \vert = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{2.44}{2} = 1.22\ \text{mV}$$ **Part (iii) SQNR:** — 3 marks $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00\ \text{dB}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Using $f_s = f_m$ instead of $f_s = 2 f_m$** — The Nyquist rate is *twice* the maximum signal frequency, not equal to it. This is the most common error; expect a **2-mark deduction**. > 2. **Forgetting the "1.76" term in SQNR** — Writing only $6.02N$ without the $1.76$ dB offset for a sinusoidal input will lose **1 mark**. State the origin: "from the $3/2$ factor for sine-wave statistics." > 3. **Confusing unipolar vs. bipolar full-scale range** — For a bipolar ADC (range $-V$ to $+V$), $V_{FS} = 2A$ (peak-to-peak), not just the peak. For a unipolar ADC, $V_{FS}$ is the total span. Mixing these up will miscalculate $\Delta$. > 4. **Mixing up baud rate and bit rate** — In binary transmission they are equal, but the examiner may award or deduct marks based on whether you explicitly state the assumption. > 5. **Manchester waveform drawing errors** — The transition for `1` and `0` is frequently swapped by students. Remember the IEEE 802.3 convention: `1` = High→Low, `0` = Low→High. (The G.E. Thomas convention reverses this — always state the convention you are using.) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Digital Signals & Coding:** - **Digital Signal:** Discrete in **both** time *and* amplitude. Represented as binary words of length $N$ bits. - **Three Conversion Stages:** **Sampling → Quantization → Coding** (in this exact order). - **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 f_m$ — sampling rate must be at least **twice** the highest signal frequency. - **Aliasing:** Irreversible artifact produced when $f_s < 2 f_m$. Prevented by anti-aliasing low-pass filter *before* the sampler. - **Number of Levels:** $L = 2^N$, where $N$ is the ADC word length. - **Step Size (Resolution):** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$. Smaller $\Delta$ means finer resolution. - **Quantization Error:** Bounded by $\pm \Delta/2$ for mid-tread/riser quantizers. - **Mean-Square Quantization Noise:** $\Delta^2/12$ — the standard KTU 2024 result for uniform quantization. - **SQNR (dB):** $6.02 N + 1.76$ dB — every extra bit adds **~6 dB** of dynamic range. - **Two's Complement Range:** $[-2^{N-1}, 2^{N-1}-1]$ — the standard signed integer representation in DSPs and microcontrollers. - **Line Codes — Quick Identification:** - *NRZ* → no mid-bit transition, low bandwidth, has DC. - *RZ* → pulse returns to zero, has DC, higher bandwidth. - *AMI* → 1s alternate polarity, no DC, good for long-distance TDM. - *Manchester* → mandatory mid-bit transition, no DC, **self-clocking**, used in Ethernet and CAN. - *Differential Manchester* → always has mid-bit transition + extra transition for 1s. - **Gray Code Property:** Adjacent codes differ in **only one bit** — used in optical encoders to prevent false reads during shaft rotation. - **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A **10-bit ADC with 0–5 V range gives 4.88 mV resolution** and **~62 dB SQNR** — adequate for most industrial temperature/pressure control loops. - **Audio Reference:** CD-quality audio = 16-bit / 44.1 kHz → **98 dB SQNR**, covering the full 20 kHz human hearing range. ---

topics.title: Data Conversion & Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Conversion & Quantization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In **Digital Control Systems**, the physical (analog) signals from the plant and the digital signals processed by the controller are fundamentally incompatible. **Data Conversion & Quantization** refers to the set of processes that bridge the analog and digital domains:

- **A/D Conversion (Analog-to-Digital):** Transforms a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code $x[k]$ using three sequential operations — **Sampling**, **Quantization**, and **Coding (Encoding)**.
- **D/A Conversion (Digital-to-Analog):** Reconstructs a continuous-time signal $\hat{x}(t)$ from the discrete digital sequence $x[k]$ (typically using a **Zero-Order Hold**).
- **Quantization:** The non-linear, many-to-one mapping process that approximates each sampled amplitude $x(kT)$ by the nearest of $2^n$ discrete levels, introducing an irreversible **quantization error** $e_q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The chain of conversion in a closed-loop digital control system is **A/D → Digital Controller → D/A → Plant**. Understanding quantization is mandatory because it is the **only inherently non-linear** step and determines the achievable precision of the entire loop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **staircase in a parking garage**:
- The car (analog signal) can be at any continuous height in the air.
- The garage has only fixed parking levels — Ground, L1, L2… each separated by a fixed height.
- **Sampling** = looking at the car’s height only at specific instants (every 5 seconds).
- **Quantization** = forcing the car to "snap" to the nearest parking level. A car that is 0.4 m above L1 jumps down to L1; one that is 0.6 m above jumps up to L2.
- The vertical gap between consecutive levels is the **resolution ($\Delta$)** of the converter. More levels = finer staircase = better accuracy.
- The difference between the car’s true height and the snapped level is the **quantization error ($e_q$)** — a permanent, irreversible loss of information.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance
- **Number of bits:** $n$ (typical: 8, 10, 12, 14, 16).
- **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** The total analog span the converter can handle, in **Volts (V)**.
- **Resolution ($\Delta$):** The step size in **V/LSB** (Volts per Least Significant Bit).
- **Sampling Period:** $T$ in **seconds (s)**.
- **Sampling Frequency:** $f_s = 1/T$ in **Hertz (Hz)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The A/D Conversion Pipeline
The conversion of an analog signal $x(t)$ to a digital word $x[k]$ happens in three logical stages:

1. **Sampling (S/H with Sample-and-Hold):**
   - The continuous signal is multiplied by an impulse train, yielding a discrete-time sequence $x(kT)$.
   - Governed by the **Shannon-Nyquist Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
   - A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit freezes the amplitude during the A/D conversion window, eliminating aperture error.

2. **Quantization (Non-linear amplitude discretization):**
   - The sampled amplitude is mapped to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels.
   - The mapping is **non-uniform** (e.g., A-law, $\mu$-law) in audio, but **uniform (linear)** in most control applications.
   - The step size is $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$ (or $\text{FSR}/(2^n - 1)$, depending on convention).

3. **Coding (Encoding):**
   - Each quantization level is assigned a unique $n$-bit binary code (usually **Natural Binary**, or **Offset Binary** for bipolar signals, or **Two's Complement** in DSP controllers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three separate steps?** Sampling discretizes **time**; Quantization discretizes **amplitude**; Coding translates amplitude into a binary word the CPU can manipulate. Only quantization introduces **irreducible error**.

### 2.2 Quantization Error Characteristics
For **uniform mid-tread (or mid-rise) quantizers**, the quantization error $e_q(k)$ is bounded:

$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

Under the standard assumption that $e_q$ is **statistically independent**, **uniformly distributed**, and **zero-mean**:

- **Mean square error (power of quantization noise):**
$$\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

- **RMS quantization noise:**
$$e_{q,rms} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

- **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) for a full-scale sinusoid:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 \;\;\text{dB}$$

This is the famous **"6 dB per bit" rule** — every additional bit of resolution improves the SQNR by approximately **6 dB**.

### 2.3 D/A Conversion & Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)
The D/A converter outputs a **staircase waveform** $\hat{x}(t)$ that holds each digital value constant over one sampling period $T$. This is mathematically modelled as a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**:

- **Transfer function (s-domain):**
$$G_h(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

- **Transfer function (z-domain):**
$$G_h(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G_h(s)\} = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{z}\cdot\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s^2}\right\} = \frac{T(z^{-1})}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

- **Frequency response:**
$$G_h(j\omega) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$$

The ZOH behaves as a **low-pass filter** with a magnitude roll-off and a **half-sample-period phase lag**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remark** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | levels | $n$ = number of bits |
| Resolution (mid-rise) | $\Delta = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^n}$ | V/LSB | Use this in KTU problems |
| Resolution (mid-tread, $2^n-1$ intervals) | $\Delta = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^n - 1}$ | V/LSB | Less common |
| Quantization error bound | $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq \Delta/2$ | V | Uniform quantizer |
| Mean-square quantization noise | $\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | V$^2$ | Statistical model |
| RMS noise | $e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | V | $\sqrt{1/12} = 1/(2\sqrt{3})$ |
| SQNR (full-scale sine) | $6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB | "6 dB per bit" rule |
| Dynamic range | $\text{DR} = 6.02\,n$ | dB | Ratio of FSR to $\Delta$ |
| ZOH s-domain | $(1 - e^{-Ts})/s$ | — | Approximation of D/A |
| ZOH z-domain | $T\,z^{-1}/(1 - z^{-1})$ | — | Used in digital design |
| Nyquist rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling freq. |
| A/D conversion time | $t_{conv} \leq T$ | s | $T$ is sampling period |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical pipe workaround:** In the table above, the absolute-value / bound expression is rendered via text (e.g., "$-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq \Delta/2$") and not as a raw `|x|` to keep the markdown table parser safe.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Motor speed control:** A 12-bit encoder/ADC with a 10 V FSR gives $\Delta = 10/4096 \approx 2.44$ mV, sufficient for sub-RPM precision in servo drives.
- **Audio (CD standard):** 16-bit, 44.1 kHz sampling → SQNR $\approx 98$ dB, the benchmark for HiFi.
- **Instrumentation & PLCs:** Often use 12- or 14-bit ADCs with $\pm 10$ V bipolar input ranges.
- **Automotive ECUs:** 10- to 12-bit ADCs are standard for sensor fusion; quantization error dominates sensor noise at low signal levels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Quantization Levels and Resolution

**Statement:** Show that an $n$-bit ADC divides the full-scale range into $2^n$ levels and that the resolution is $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$.

**Step 1 — Counting binary words.** An $n$-bit binary number can represent exactly $2^n$ distinct combinations, ranging from $0$ to $2^n - 1$.

**Step 2 — Mapping to amplitude.** Each combination corresponds to one quantization level, so the number of levels is:

$$L = 2^n$$

**Step 3 — Defining resolution.** Resolution is the smallest distinguishable change in analog input, which equals the spacing between adjacent levels. With $L$ levels covering FSR, the spacing is:

$$\Delta = \frac{\text{FSR}}{L} = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n}$$

**Step 4 — Worked numeric example (KTU-style).** For an 8-bit ADC with FSR = 10 V:

$$L = 2^8 = 256 \text{ levels}, \quad \Delta = \frac{10}{256} = 0.0390625 \text{ V} = 39.0625 \text{ mV}$$

If upgraded to 12 bits:

$$L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}, \quad \Delta = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.44 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Quantization error bound.** Because the true analog value can lie anywhere within one interval of width $\Delta$, the maximum error of the mid-rise quantizer is bounded:

$$e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^{n+1}}$$

> This is the **error budget** the designer must allocate for A/D conversion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean-Square Quantization Noise and SQNR

**Statement:** For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$ and statistically uniform error distribution, derive the RMS noise and the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio for a full-scale sinusoidal input.

**Step 1 — Error distribution.** Assume $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$. The probability density function is $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$ over that interval.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean (zero by symmetry):**

$$\mu_{e_q} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} \frac{e_q}{\Delta}\, de_q = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean-square (variance of noise):**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot \left[\frac{e_q^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot \frac{2(\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — RMS noise voltage:**

$$e_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\overline{e_q^2}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Signal power (full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$).** The RMS signal value is $A/\sqrt{2}$, and the "full-scale" peak is $A = \text{FSR}/2$, so:

$$P_{signal} = \left(\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{\text{FSR}^2}{8}$$

**Step 6 — Noise power in terms of FSR and $n$:** Since $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$:

$$P_{noise} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{1}{12}\cdot \frac{\text{FSR}^2}{2^{2n}}$$

**Step 7 — SQNR ratio:**

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{\text{FSR}^2/8}{\text{FSR}^2/(12\cdot 2^{2n})} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2n}}{8} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2n} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to decibels:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 20n\log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02\,n \;\;\text{dB}$$

> This is the canonical result: each extra bit of resolution buys ~6 dB of dynamic range.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: ZOH Transfer Function in the z-Domain

**Statement:** Derive the pulse transfer function of a Zero-Order Hold used at the output of a digital controller.

**Step 1 — ZOH impulse response.** The ZOH outputs a rectangular pulse of height equal to the input sample, of width $T$:

$$h(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)$$

where $u(t)$ is the unit step.

**Step 2 — Laplace transform:**

$$G_h(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to z-domain.** The factor $1 - e^{-Ts}$ suggests splitting:

$$G_h(s) = \left(1 - e^{-Ts}\right)\cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

In the z-domain, multiplication by $e^{-Ts}$ corresponds to a one-sample delay, i.e. multiplication by $z^{-1}$. The $1/s$ block is the integrator, whose sampled output is a ramp $kT$ (Z-transform: $Tz/(z-1)^2$).

**Step 4 — Compose:**

$$G_h(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\cdot \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s^2}\right\} = (1 - z^{-1})\cdot \frac{Tz}{(z - 1)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z$:

$$G_h(z) = \frac{T(z - 1)}{(z - 1)^2}\cdot z^{-1}\cdot z = \frac{T\,z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{T}{z - 1}$$

Both forms $G_h(z) = Tz^{-1}/(1 - z^{-1})$ and $G_h(z) = T/(z-1)$ are accepted in KTU solutions.

> **Engineering insight:** $T/(z-1)$ is the discrete-time equivalent of the analog integrator $1/s$. The factor $z^{-1}$ represents the inherent one-step delay introduced by any real hold device — a critical point in digital controller stability analysis.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: A/D vs D/A Conversion Trade-offs

| **Parameter** | **A/D Converter (ADC)** | **D/A Converter (DAC)** | **KTU Board Perspective** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | Continuous → Discrete | Discrete → Continuous | Both needed in a digital loop |
| Key non-ideality | Quantization error | Aperture / zero-order hold | Both degrade performance |
| Typical bits (control) | 10 – 16 | 10 – 16 | Match ADC/DAC to avoid bottleneck |
| Conversion time | $t_{conv} \leq T$ | Settling time $\ll T$ | Limits achievable bandwidth |
| Linearity metric | INL, DNL (LSB) | INL, DNL (LSB) | Specified in datasheets |
| Output form | Binary code (e.g., 2's complement) | Staircase waveform (held) | DAC rarely "smooth" |
| Filter needed after | Anti-aliasing (pre) | Reconstruction (post) | Both are analog LPFs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Data Conversion Block Diagram in a Digital Control Loop
The following Mermaid block diagram shows the canonical signal flow in a KTU-style digital control system, with explicit annotation of every conversion step.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input r-t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter LPF]
    B --> C[Sampler and Hold S/H]
    C --> D[Quantizer Q-n-bit]
    D --> E[Encoder Binary Code]
    E --> F[Digital Controller D-z]
    F --> G[DAC with ZOH]
    G --> H[Plant G-s]
    H --> I[Sensor H-s]
    I --> J[Feedback Summer]
    J --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Anti-Aliasing Filter** is a continuous-time LPF with cutoff $f_c < f_s/2$.
- The **S/H** holds the analog value constant during the A/D conversion aperture.
- The **Quantizer** is the **only non-linear** block — this is the focus of Module 1.
- The **ZOH** after the DAC introduces a half-sample phase lag and a sinc-shaped magnitude roll-off.

---

### 4.2 Quantization Error Generation Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Continuous Signal x-t] --> S[Sampler: x-kT]
    S --> Q[Quantizer: 2 to the n levels]
    Q --> XQ[Digital Output: x-hat-k]
    S --> SUB((Subtraction Node))
    XQ --> SUB
    SUB --> E[Quantization Error e-q-k]
    Q -.->|non-linear, many-to-one| E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key idea:** $x[k] = Q\{x(kT)\}$, and $e_q[k] = Q\{x(kT)\} - x(kT)$. Because $Q(\cdot)$ is non-linear, a true LTI (linear time-invariant) analysis is impossible. The standard KTU approach is to **replace the quantizer by an additive white noise source** $e_q[k]$ for performance analysis — a justified approximation when the input is "busy enough" to traverse many levels.

---

### 4.3 Zero-Order Hold Waveform Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DAC Input Samples: 2.1, 3.5, 1.2, 4.0] --> B[ZOH Staircase Output]
    B --> C[Continuous Plant Input]
    subgraph time_grid
        T0["t=0"] --> T1["t=T"] --> T2["t=2T"] --> T3["t=3T"]
    end
```

The ZOH holds the digital value $x[k]$ constant over the interval $[kT,\,(k+1)T)$, producing a staircase. The vertical jumps at multiples of $T$ are smoothed by the plant's own low-pass dynamics and by an optional **reconstruction filter** at the output.

---

### 4.4 Resolution vs Number of Bits — Trade-off Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0["n = 8 bits"] --> L0["L = 256"]
    N1["n = 10 bits"] --> L1["L = 1024"]
    N2["n = 12 bits"] --> L2["L = 4096"]
    N3["n = 14 bits"] --> L3["L = 16384"]
    N4["n = 16 bits"] --> L4["L = 65536"]
    L0 --> S0["Delta = 39.06 mV for 10 V FSR"]
    L1 --> S1["Delta = 9.77 mV for 10 V FSR"]
    L2 --> S2["Delta = 2.44 mV for 10 V FSR"]
    L3 --> S3["Delta = 0.61 mV for 10 V FSR"]
    L4 --> S4["Delta = 0.153 mV for 10 V FSR"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define quantization error. Derive the expression for the mean-square value of the quantization error of a uniform quantizer.*

**Model Answer:**
Quantization error is the difference between the actual sampled analog value $x(kT)$ and the quantized (digitized) output $x_Q(k)$ of the A/D converter:
$$e_q(k) = x_Q(k) - x(kT)$$
For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$, the error is bounded by $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq \Delta/2$. Assuming the error is uniformly distributed in this interval with pdf $p(e_q)=1/\Delta$, the mean is zero and the mean-square value is:
$$\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} \frac{e_q^2}{\Delta}\,de_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$
This is the **quantization noise power** in V².

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem. Why is an anti-aliasing filter required before the sampler?*

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states that a band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(kT)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The minimum rate $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist rate**.
An **anti-aliasing filter** (analog low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$) is required before the sampler to remove frequency components above $f_s/2$. Without it, **aliasing** occurs: high-frequency components fold back into the baseband and become indistinguishable from the true low-frequency content, causing permanent loss of information.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the process of Analog-to-Digital (A/D) conversion with a neat block diagram. List the three operations involved and state the role of the Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC has a unipolar input range of 0 to 10 V. Calculate:
- (i) the number of quantization levels,
- (ii) the resolution in mV/LSB,
- (iii) the maximum quantization error in mV,
- (iv) the SQNR in dB for a full-scale sinusoidal input. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
The A/D conversion process converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal $x(t)$ into a discrete digital word $x[k]$ using three sequential operations:
1. **Sampling:** The signal is sampled at uniform intervals $T$ by a Sample-and-Hold circuit, producing $x(kT)$.
2. **Quantization:** Each sample is mapped to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels.
3. **Encoding:** Each level is assigned a unique $n$-bit binary code.

**Role of the S/H circuit:** During the time taken by the A/D converter to resolve the digital code (the **aperture time**), the analog input may change. The S/H freezes the input amplitude to a constant value, ensuring a stable, unambiguous conversion and eliminating **aperture error**.

**Model Solution (b):**
Given: $n = 12$ bits, FSR = 10 V.

**(i) Number of quantization levels:**
$$L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

**(ii) Resolution (step size):**
$$\Delta = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.4414 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**(iii) Maximum quantization error:**
$$e_{q,\max} = \pm\frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm 1.2207 \text{ mV}$$

**(iv) SQNR for a full-scale sinusoid:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02\times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) used in D/A conversion. Derive its transfer function in the s-domain and z-domain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10-bit ADC quantizes a full-scale sine wave of peak amplitude 5 V. Compute:
- (i) the RMS signal voltage,
- (ii) the RMS quantization noise voltage,
- (iii) the SQNR in dB (verify using the formula and by direct ratio calculation). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
The ZOH is a device that holds the digital input constant for the duration of one sampling period $T$, producing a staircase output.

**Block diagram (textual):**
Digital word $x[k]$ → DAC latch → ZOH circuit → Staircase output $\hat{x}(t)$ → Reconstruction filter → Plant input.

**Derivation of s-domain transfer function:**
The ZOH impulse response is $h(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step.
$$G_h(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

**Derivation of z-domain transfer function:**
Factor as $G_h(s) = (1 - e^{-Ts})\cdot(1/s^2)$. The factor $1/s^2$ corresponds to a ramp, whose Z-transform is $Tz/(z-1)^2$, and $1 - e^{-Ts}$ corresponds to $1 - z^{-1}$. Therefore:
$$G_h(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\cdot\frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{T\,z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{T}{z - 1}$$

**Model Solution (b):**
Given: $n = 10$ bits, $A = 5$ V (peak), FSR $\geq 2A = 10$ V (take FSR = 10 V).

**(i) RMS signal voltage (full-scale sine):**
$$V_{rms} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 3.5355 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) RMS quantization noise:**
$$\Delta = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{1024} \approx 9.7656 \text{ mV}$$
$$e_{q,rms} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{9.7656 \text{ mV}}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 2.8192 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) SQNR by direct ratio:**
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{e_{q,rms}^2} = \left(\frac{3.5355}{0.0028192}\right)^2 \approx 1.572 \times 10^6$$
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(1.572\times 10^6) \approx 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

**Verification by formula:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02\times 10 + 1.76 = 60.20 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

Both methods agree to within rounding, confirming the result.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Resolution formula confusion:** Some students write $\Delta = \text{FSR}/(2^n - 1)$ and some write $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$. The KTU board uses the **mid-rise (or "no missing code" standard)** convention $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$. Always state the convention.
> 2. **Confusing peak with RMS:** A full-scale sine has peak $A$ and RMS $A/\sqrt{2}$. The signal power used in the SQNR formula assumes RMS, not peak. Losing 3 dB is a common error.
> 3. **Forgetting units in resolution:** Report $\Delta$ in **mV/LSB**, not raw volts. The board specifically checks for unit consistency.
> 4. **Skipping the anti-aliasing filter mention:** Any block diagram of an A/D pipeline without an anti-aliasing filter is marked incomplete.
> 5. **Wrong quantizer model:** Students often treat the quantizer as linear. Always note that it is **non-linear** and justify the use of the statistical noise model.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **A/D pipeline:** Sampling → Quantization → Encoding (S/H is part of sampling).
- **Number of levels:** $L = 2^n$ for an $n$-bit converter.
- **Resolution (mid-rise):** $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^n$, in **V/LSB**.
- **Quantization error bound:** $\pm \Delta/2$.
- **Mean-square quantization noise:** $\Delta^2/12$ (V²).
- **RMS quantization noise:** $\Delta/(2\sqrt{3})$ (V).
- **SQNR rule of thumb:** $6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB (for full-scale sine) — "**6 dB per bit**."
- **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Anti-aliasing filter:** Analog LPF with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$ is mandatory before the sampler.
- **D/A + ZOH:** Output is a staircase; the ZOH transfer function in z-domain is $G_h(z) = T/(z-1)$.
- **Inherent ZOH delay:** $T/2$ second phase lag — must be accounted for in stability margins (gain/phase margin shrinks).
- **Statistical noise assumption:** Quantization error is treated as zero-mean, white, uniform; this is valid only when the input is sufficiently "active" (e.g., not stuck at a single level).
- **Dynamic range:** $\text{DR} = 6.02\,n$ dB (ratio of FSR to $\Delta$).
- **Conversion time constraint:** $t_{conv} < T$ — the ADC must resolve the digital word within one sampling period.
- **Encoder types:** Natural Binary (unipolar), Offset Binary (bipolar), Two's Complement (DSP).
- **Why ZOH is preferred over First-Order Hold (FOH):** Simpler, cheaper, more stable, and sufficient for most control loops. FOH gives better reconstruction at the cost of noise amplification.
- **Aperture error:** The error introduced if the S/H is absent and the input changes during conversion. Always include an S/H in high-speed or high-accuracy systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision mnemonic — "Q-S-N-R":** **Q**uantization error squared, divided by **S**ample size gives **N**oise power; **R**MS is its square root. This single sentence gets you through 70% of Module 1 numerical questions on the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
