> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mapping from S-Plane to Z-Plane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In Digital Control Systems, the relationship between the continuous-time **s-plane** (Laplace domain) and the discrete-time **z-plane** (Z-transform domain) is established through the **exponential mapping** governed by the sampling process. The fundamental mapping equation is:

$$z = e^{sT}$$

where:
- $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the complex variable of the Laplace transform (continuous-time frequency)
- $T$ is the **sampling period** in seconds (also written as $T_s$)
- $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ is the **sampling frequency** in rad/sec

The inverse mapping is given by:

$$s = \dfrac{1}{T}\ln(z)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ is the cornerstone of every discrete-time analysis. It defines how poles/zeros of $G(s)$ translate into poles/zeros of $G(z)$ and establishes the stability boundary between the two domains.

## 1.2 Magnitude and Angle Interpretation

Substituting $s = \sigma + j\omega$ into the mapping equation:

$$z = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T} = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$$

This cleanly separates into two polar coordinates in the z-plane:

| z-plane Parameter | Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Magnitude (modulus) | $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ | Determined only by the real part $\sigma$ |
| Angle (argument) | $\angle z = \omega T$ | Determined only by the imaginary part $\omega$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Folding Carpet"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Accordion-Fold Analogy**
> Imagine the entire infinite **s-plane** as a long, flat carpet. The **z-plane** (specifically the unit disk $\vert z \vert \le 1$) is a small circular rug. The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ acts like an accordion-fold: every horizontal strip of the s-plane of width $\dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ (centered at $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi k}{T}$ for integer $k$) gets compressed and laid on top of the unit disk. The **primary strip** ($-\dfrac{\pi}{T} \le \omega \le \dfrac{\pi}{T}$) maps bijectively onto the entire z-plane — this is the only strip that survives without aliasing.

## 1.4 Mapping of Critical Axes — The Stability Bridge

| Region in s-plane | Condition | Maps to | z-plane Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Left Half Plane (LHP) | $\sigma < 0$ | $\vert z \vert < 1$ | **Inside** the unit circle → **Stable** |
| Imaginary Axis ($j\omega$-axis) | $\sigma = 0$ | $\vert z \vert = 1$ | **On** the unit circle → Marginally stable |
| Right Half Plane (RHP) | $\sigma > 0$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | **Outside** the unit circle → **Unstable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Equivalence Rule:** A continuous-time system is BIBO stable iff all its poles lie in the LHP. After sampling, the equivalent digital system is BIBO stable iff **all its poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** of the z-plane. This is the **single most important takeaway** of this topic for KTU exams.

## 1.5 The Primary Strip — Why $\pm \pi/T$ Matters

The imaginary axis of the s-plane spans $-\infty < \omega < +\infty$. However, due to the periodicity of $e^{j\omega T}$, the angle $\omega T$ is periodic with period $2\pi$. Therefore, the entire $j\omega$-axis maps onto the unit circle **infinitely many times**, once for each strip:

$$\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{T} \le \omega \le \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{T}, \quad k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$

The strip with $k = 0$, i.e., $-\dfrac{\pi}{T} \le \omega \le \dfrac{\pi}{T}$, is called the **primary strip** (or principal strip). Frequencies beyond $\omega_s/2 = \pi/T$ alias back into this strip — this is the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling limit**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sampling Bridge — Why Does $z = e^{sT}$ Hold?

A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is sampled by an ideal impulse sampler producing:

$$x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\delta(t - kT)$$

Taking the Laplace transform of $x^*(t)$:

$$X^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\, e^{-skT}$$

Recognising this as the definition of the **Z-transform** with $z = e^{sT}$, the discrete and continuous transforms become identical under this substitution — hence the canonical mapping $z = e^{sT}$.

## 2.2 Properties of the Mapping — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Magnitude depends only on $\sigma$:**
$\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$, so the **real part** of $s$ governs **distance from origin** in $z$.

**Step 2 — Angle depends only on $\omega$:**
$\angle z = \omega T$, so the **imaginary part** of $s$ (modulo $2\pi$) governs **angular position** in $z$.

**Step 3 — Vertical lines $\sigma =$ const become circles:**
A constant real part $\sigma = a$ maps to a circle of radius $r = e^{aT}$ centered at origin.

**Step 4 — Horizontal lines $\omega =$ const become radial rays:**
A constant imaginary part $\omega = b$ maps to a straight line from origin at angle $\theta = bT$.

**Step 5 — LHP boundary maps to unit circle:**
$\sigma = 0$ gives $r = e^0 = 1$ — the stability boundary in z-plane.

## 2.3 Mapping of Special Curves — High-Yield for KTU

### (A) Constant Damping Ratio Lines ($\zeta =$ constant)

Lines in the s-plane passing through the origin at angle $\phi$ from the negative real axis where $\cos\phi = \zeta$.

In the s-plane: $\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega} = -\dfrac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ (negative slope since $\sigma < 0$)

In the z-plane, using $\sigma T = \ln r$ and $\omega T = \theta$:

$$\boxed{\,r = e^{-\dfrac{\zeta\,\theta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}\,}$$

This is a **logarithmic spiral** in the z-plane (because $\ln r$ is linear in $\theta$).

### (B) Constant Natural Frequency Lines ($\omega_n =$ constant)

Circles in the s-plane: $\sigma^2 + \omega^2 = \omega_n^2$.

Substituting $\sigma = \dfrac{\ln r}{T}$ and $\omega = \dfrac{\theta}{T}$:

$$\boxed{\,(\ln r)^2 + \theta^2 = (\omega_n T)^2\,}$$

This is a **circle in the $(\ln r, \theta)$ plane** of radius $\omega_n T$ — it appears as an **ellipse-like closed curve** when plotted in the actual $(r, \theta)$ plane.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z = e^{sT}$ | Fundamental mapping equation |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{1}{T}\ln z$ | Inverse mapping |
| 3 | $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ | Magnitude depends only on $\sigma$ |
| 4 | $\angle z = \omega T$ | Angle depends only on $\omega$ |
| 5 | $\omega = \Omega T$ | Analog freq $\Omega$ (rad/s) ↔ digital freq $\omega$ (rad/sample) |
| 6 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Sampling frequency in rad/s |
| 7 | $\omega_N = \dfrac{\omega_s}{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{T}$ | Nyquist frequency |
| 8 | LHP ↔ $\vert z \vert < 1$ | Stability equivalence |
| 9 | $j\omega$-axis ↔ $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Marginal stability |
| 10 | RHP ↔ $\vert z \vert > 1$ | Instability |
| 11 | Primary strip: $-\dfrac{\pi}{T} \le \omega \le \dfrac{\pi}{T}$ | Only non-aliased region |
| 12 | $r = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\zeta\,\theta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right)$ | Constant-$\zeta$ curve (log-spiral) |
| 13 | $(\ln r)^2 + \theta^2 = (\omega_n T)^2$ | Constant-$\omega_n$ curve |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The s-to-z mapping is used in designing **digital controllers** for power-electronic converters, robotic arms, and aerospace flight systems. A designer tunes a PID in the s-plane using familiar classical concepts (damping, natural frequency) and then converts to z-plane via $z = e^{sT}$ for implementation on a microprocessor or DSP.

## 2.5 Frequency Warping — A Subtle Effect

If the discrete system has sampling period $T$, a true analog frequency $\Omega$ (rad/s) appears as:

$$\omega_d = \Omega T \quad \text{(rad/sample)}$$

and the analog frequency $\Omega$ is reconstructed as:

$$\Omega = \frac{\omega_d}{T}$$

This **scaling** is linear and does not distort the spectrum shape — but the **non-linear logarithmic mapping** $\ln z$ does introduce warping when designing controllers via bilinear (Tustin) transformation, which is a separate (advanced) topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Sampled Laplace Transform to $z = e^{sT}$

**Starting point:** Consider a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ sampled periodically at instants $t = 0, T, 2T, \ldots, kT$.

The sampled signal, represented as a train of impulses, is:

$$x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$$

Taking the **Laplace transform** of this impulse train (using the sifting property $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t-a)\} = e^{-as}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
X^*(s) &= \mathcal{L}\{x^*(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)\, e^{-st}\, dt \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \int_{0}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)\, e^{-st}\, dt \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\, e^{-skT}
\end{aligned}$$

(Sifting property: $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t-a)\,dt = f(a)$.)

Now define a new variable $z$ such that $z = e^{sT}$. Then $e^{-skT} = (e^{sT})^{-k} = z^{-k}$.

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\, z^{-k}
\end{aligned}$$

This is exactly the **one-sided Z-transform definition** of $x(kT)$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,X(z) \equiv X^*(s)\,\Big\vert_{z = e^{sT}}\quad \Rightarrow \quad z = e^{sT}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polar Form of $z$ in Terms of $s$

Let $s = \sigma + j\omega$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
z &= e^{sT} = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T} \\
&= e^{\sigma T}\cdot e^{j\omega T}
\end{aligned}$$

By Euler's formula, $e^{j\omega T} = \cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)$. So $z$ in rectangular coordinates:

$$z = e^{\sigma T}\cos(\omega T) + j\,e^{\sigma T}\sin(\omega T)$$

In polar form:

$$\boxed{\,\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}, \qquad \arg(z) = \omega T\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mapping of Constant Damping Ratio Line ($\zeta =$ const)

A constant damping ratio line in the s-plane is a straight line through the origin making an angle $\phi$ with the **negative real axis**, where $\zeta = \cos\phi$.

On this line, the relationship between $\sigma$ and $\omega$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\zeta &= \cos\phi = \frac{-\sigma}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \omega^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for the ratio $\sigma/\omega$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\zeta^2 &= \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 + \omega^2} \\
\zeta^2(\sigma^2 + \omega^2) &= \sigma^2 \\
\zeta^2\,\omega^2 &= \sigma^2(1 - \zeta^2) \\
\frac{\sigma}{\omega} &= \pm\frac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

For the **LHP** side of the line (stable region), $\sigma < 0$ and $\omega > 0$, giving:

$$\frac{\sigma}{\omega} = -\frac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

Now apply the mapping. From the polar form derived in §3.2:

$$\sigma = \frac{\ln r}{T}, \qquad \omega = \frac{\theta}{T}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{(\ln r)/T}{\theta/T} &= -\frac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \\
\frac{\ln r}{\theta} &= -\frac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \\
\ln r &= -\frac{\zeta\,\theta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\boxed{\,r(\theta) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\zeta\,\theta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}\right)\,}$$

**Geometric interpretation:** Since $\ln r$ is linear in $\theta$, this curve is a **logarithmic spiral** in the z-plane. As $\zeta \to 1$, the spiral collapses toward the positive real axis; as $\zeta \to 0$, it becomes the unit circle (vertical line $\sigma = 0$ in s-plane).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mapping of Constant Natural Frequency ($\omega_n =$ const)

A constant natural frequency corresponds to a circle of radius $\omega_n$ in the s-plane:

$$\sigma^2 + \omega^2 = \omega_n^2$$

Applying the same substitution $\sigma = \dfrac{\ln r}{T}$ and $\omega = \dfrac{\theta}{T}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\ln r}{T}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\theta}{T}\right)^2 &= \omega_n^2 \\
\frac{(\ln r)^2}{T^2} + \frac{\theta^2}{T^2} &= \omega_n^2 \\
(\ln r)^2 + \theta^2 &= (\omega_n T)^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,(\ln r)^2 + \theta^2 = (\omega_n T)^2\,}$$

**Geometric interpretation:** This is a **circle of radius $\omega_n T$** in the $(\ln r, \theta)$ plane. When plotted in the actual polar $(r, \theta)$ plane of the z-plane, this circle transforms into a closed **ellipse-like curve** (technically not a true ellipse due to the $\ln$ transformation). Designers use these loci to translate desired $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$ specifications from s-plane to z-plane.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A continuous-time system has poles at $s_1 = -1 + j2$ and $s_2 = -1 - j2$. The sampling period is $T = 0.5$ sec. Find the corresponding poles in the z-plane.

**Solution:**

For $s_1 = -1 + j2$: $\sigma_1 = -1$, $\omega_1 = 2$

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert z_1 \vert &= e^{\sigma_1 T} = e^{(-1)(0.5)} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065 \\
\angle z_1 &= \omega_1 T = (2)(0.5) = 1.0 \text{ rad} \approx 57.3^\circ \\
z_1 &= 0.6065 \angle 57.3^\circ \\
&= 0.6065\cos(1.0) + j\,0.6065\sin(1.0) \\
&= 0.6065(0.5403) + j\,0.6065(0.8415) \\
&\approx 0.3277 + j\,0.5104
\end{aligned}$$

For $s_2 = -1 - j2$ (complex conjugate):

$$z_2 = z_1^* \approx 0.3277 - j\,0.5104$$

**Stability check:** Both $\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert = 0.6065 < 1$ ✓ — the discrete system is **stable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** The continuous pole in LHP ($\sigma < 0$) produces $\vert z \vert < 1$, which is the discrete stability condition. This is the canonical method to discretize continuous poles.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Summary

| s-plane feature | Equation | Maps to (z-plane) | Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| Imaginary axis | $\sigma = 0$ | $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Unit circle |
| Real axis (positive) | $\omega = 0, \sigma > 0$ | $r > 1, \theta = 0$ | Positive real ray outside unit circle |
| Real axis (negative) | $\omega = 0, \sigma < 0$ | $r < 1, \theta = 0$ | Positive real ray inside unit circle |
| Vertical line $\sigma = a$ | $\sigma = a, \omega \in (-\infty,\infty)$ | $r = e^{aT}, \theta = \omega T$ | Concentric circle |
| Horizontal line $\omega = b$ | $\omega = b, \sigma \in (-\infty,\infty)$ | $\theta = bT, r = e^{\sigma T}$ | Radial ray from origin |
| Constant $\zeta$ line | $\sigma = -\zeta\omega/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | $r = \exp(-\zeta\theta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$ | Logarithmic spiral |
| Constant $\omega_n$ circle | $\sigma^2 + \omega^2 = \omega_n^2$ | $(\ln r)^2 + \theta^2 = (\omega_n T)^2$ | Spiral-like closed curve |
| Primary strip | $-\pi/T \le \omega \le \pi/T$ | Entire z-plane | Bijection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Mapping Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the conceptual pipeline of how a continuous-time design is transformed into its discrete-time implementation through the s-to-z mapping.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CONTINUOUS PLANT Gs"] --> B["DESIGN IN S PLANE"]
    B["DESIGN IN S PLANE"] --> C["SPECIFY DESIRED POLES sigma omega"]
    C["SPECIFY DESIRED POLES sigma omega"] --> D["APPLY MAPPING z equals e to power sT"]
    D["APPLY MAPPING z equals e to power sT"] --> E["COMPUTE Z PLANE POLES mod and angle"]
    E["COMPUTE Z PLANE POLES mod and angle"] --> F["FORM DIGITAL COMPENSATOR DZ"]
    F["FORM DIGITAL COMPENSATOR DZ"] --> G["IMPLEMENT ON MICROCONTROLLER OR DSP"]
    G["IMPLEMENT ON MICROCONTROLLER OR DSP"] --> H["SAMPLE PLANT OUTPUT ykT"]
    H["SAMPLE PLANT OUTPUT ykT"] --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The continuous plant $G(s)$ is studied in the s-plane first.
- Desired pole locations ($\sigma, \omega$) are mapped via $z = e^{sT}$ to obtain discrete pole positions.
- The digital compensator $D(z)$ is built and downloaded to a microcontroller/DSP.
- The output $y(kT)$ is sampled and fed back to close the loop.

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping Diagram — Curves in s-Plane to z-Plane

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPLANE["S PLANE CONTINUOUS DOMAIN"]
        S1["IMAGINARY AXIS sigma equals 0"]
        S2["LHP sigma less than 0 STABLE REGION"]
        S3["RHP sigma greater than 0 UNSTABLE REGION"]
        S4["CONSTANT ZETA LINE THROUGH ORIGIN"]
        S5["CONSTANT OMEGA n CIRCLE"]
        S6["PRIMARY STRIP minus pi by T to pi by T"]
    end
    subgraph ZPLANE["Z PLANE DISCRETE DOMAIN"]
        Z1["UNIT CIRCLE mod z equals 1"]
        Z2["INSIDE UNIT CIRCLE mod z less than 1 STABLE"]
        Z3["OUTSIDE UNIT CIRCLE mod z greater than 1 UNSTABLE"]
        Z4["LOGARITHMIC SPIRAL"]
        Z5["CLOSED ELLIPSE LIKE CURVE"]
        Z6["ENTIRE Z PLANE"]
    end
    S1 -- "z equals e power sT" --> Z1
    S2 -- "mod z equals e power sigma T less than 1" --> Z2
    S3 -- "mod z greater than 1" --> Z3
    S4 -- "r equals exp minus zeta theta by root 1 minus zeta squared" --> Z4
    S5 -- "ln r squared plus theta squared equals omega n T squared" --> Z5
    S6 -- "bijective coverage" --> Z6
```

**Interpretation:**
- The s-plane is partitioned by stability boundaries (axes), damping characteristics ($\zeta$ lines), and frequency characteristics ($\omega_n$ circles).
- Each characteristic feature transforms into a unique geometric figure in the z-plane under the mapping $z = e^{sT}$.
- The **primary strip** is the only region of the s-plane that maps **one-to-one** onto the z-plane — outside it, aliasing causes multiple s-plane points to coincide at the same z-plane location.

---

## 4.3 Stability Region Correspondence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LHP["S PLANE LEFT HALF sigma less than 0"]
        L1["POLE AT s equals minus 1 plus j2"]
    end
    subgraph IUC["Z PLANE INSIDE UNIT CIRCLE mod z less than 1"]
        I1["POLE AT z equals 0.6065 angle 57.3 degrees"]
    end
    LHP -->|"z equals e power sT equals e power minus 0.5 times angle 1.0"| IUC
```

This single-arrow visualisation captures the core intuition: **a pole in the LHP of the s-plane always lands inside the unit circle of the z-plane**, ensuring stability is preserved through the sampling process (assuming the sampling rate satisfies the Nyquist criterion).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 3 Marks

**Q: Define the mapping from s-plane to z-plane. Explain how the $j\omega$-axis of the s-plane maps onto the z-plane.**

**Model Answer:**

The mapping between the s-plane and z-plane is given by the fundamental equation:

$$z = e^{sT}$$

where $T$ is the sampling period.

For points on the imaginary axis of the s-plane, $s = j\omega$ (i.e., $\sigma = 0$). Substituting:

$$z = e^{j\omega T}$$

The magnitude becomes $\vert z \vert = e^{0 \cdot T} = 1$ and the angle is $\angle z = \omega T$.

Therefore, the **entire $j\omega$-axis** maps onto the **unit circle** in the z-plane. Because the angle $\omega T$ is periodic with period $2\pi$, the imaginary axis wraps around the unit circle **infinitely many times** as $\omega$ varies from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$. Each horizontal strip of width $2\pi/T$ in the s-plane covers the unit circle exactly once.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 3 Marks

**Q: What is the primary strip in the s-to-z mapping? Why is it significant?**

**Model Answer:**

The **primary strip** (or principal strip) is the horizontal strip in the s-plane bounded by:

$$-\frac{\pi}{T} \le \omega \le +\frac{\pi}{T}$$

centered on the real axis ($\sigma$-axis).

**Significance:**
1. **Bijective (one-to-one) mapping:** The primary strip maps uniquely onto the entire z-plane — every point inside the strip corresponds to a unique point in the z-plane and vice versa.
2. **No aliasing:** Frequencies within this strip are below the Nyquist frequency $\omega_N = \pi/T$, so no aliasing occurs.
3. **Frequency interpretation:** Any analog frequency $\Omega$ (in rad/s) corresponds to a digital frequency $\omega_d = \Omega T$ (in rad/sample) lying in the range $[-\pi, +\pi]$, which is exactly the angle range of one full revolution around the unit circle.
4. **Design convenience:** Designers restrict their attention to this strip because outside it, the mapping is many-to-one and the discrete model cannot distinguish the original analog frequencies.

---

## PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive the fundamental mapping equation $z = e^{sT}$ from the sampled Laplace transform. Hence obtain the magnitude and angle of $z$ in terms of $\sigma$ and $\omega$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sampled-data control system has the characteristic equation:

$$z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5 = 0$$

Determine whether the system is stable. If not, comment on the location of the poles relative to the unit circle. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Consider the continuous signal $x(t)$ sampled at $t = kT$, producing the impulse train:

$$x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$$

**Step 2:** Take the Laplace transform:

$$X^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\, e^{-skT} \quad \text{(using sifting property)}$$

**Step 3:** Define $z = e^{sT}$, so $e^{-skT} = z^{-k}$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\, z^{-k}$$

This is the Z-transform definition. Hence $\boxed{z = e^{sT}}$.

**Step 4:** Substituting $s = \sigma + j\omega$:

$$z = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T} = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude and angle:

$$\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}, \qquad \angle z = \omega T$$

**Step 6:** Stability implication — for $\sigma < 0$ (LHP), $\vert z \vert < 1$, so the **inside of the unit circle** corresponds to the stable region.

---

#### Part (b) — Stability Check (7 Marks)

Given: $z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5 = 0$.

Using the quadratic formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
z &= \frac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{(1.2)^2 - 4(1)(0.5)}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{1.44 - 2.0}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{-0.56}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \pm j\,0.7483}{2} \\
&= 0.6 \pm j\,0.3742
\end{aligned}$$

Compute the magnitude of each pole:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert &= \sqrt{(0.6)^2 + (0.3742)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.36 + 0.1400} \\
&= \sqrt{0.5000} \\
&= 0.7071
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert = 0.7071 < 1$, both poles lie **inside the unit circle**.

**Conclusion:** The system is **stable**. ✅

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the mapping of (i) constant damping ratio ($\zeta$) lines and (ii) constant natural frequency ($\omega_n$) circles from the s-plane to the z-plane. Derive the relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-time system has dominant poles at $s = -2 \pm j4$. If the sampling period is $T = 0.2$ sec, determine the corresponding discrete-time pole locations and verify the stability of the digital system. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Mapping of $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$ Curves (7 Marks)

**(i) Constant $\zeta$ mapping:**

A constant damping ratio line in the s-plane makes an angle $\phi$ with the negative real axis, where $\zeta = \cos\phi$. On this line:

$$\frac{\sigma}{\omega} = -\frac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

Using $\sigma = \ln r / T$ and $\omega = \theta / T$:

$$\ln r = -\frac{\zeta\,\theta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

$$\boxed{r(\theta) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\zeta\,\theta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}\right)}$$

This is a **logarithmic spiral** in the z-plane.

**(ii) Constant $\omega_n$ mapping:**

The circle $\sigma^2 + \omega^2 = \omega_n^2$ in the s-plane, after substitution:

$$\boxed{(\ln r)^2 + \theta^2 = (\omega_n T)^2}$$

This is a **circle in the $(\ln r, \theta)$ plane** of radius $\omega_n T$, appearing as an ellipse-like closed curve in the actual $(r, \theta)$ plane.

---

#### Part (b) — Discrete Pole Computation (7 Marks)

Given: $s_{1,2} = -2 \pm j4$, $T = 0.2$ sec.

**For $s_1 = -2 + j4$:** $\sigma = -2$, $\omega = 4$

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert z_1 \vert &= e^{\sigma T} = e^{(-2)(0.2)} = e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703 \\
\angle z_1 &= \omega T = (4)(0.2) = 0.8 \text{ rad} \approx 45.84^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

Rectangular form:

$$\begin{aligned}
z_1 &= 0.6703\cos(0.8) + j\,0.6703\sin(0.8) \\
&= 0.6703(0.6967) + j\,0.6703(0.7174) \\
&\approx 0.4670 + j\,0.4808
\end{aligned}$$

**For $s_2 = -2 - j4$ (complex conjugate):**

$$z_2 \approx 0.4670 - j\,0.4808$$

**Stability check:** $\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert \approx 0.6703 < 1$ ✓

Both discrete poles lie **inside the unit circle**, so the **digital system is stable**. ✅

**Damping verification:** Original $\zeta = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{2^2 + 4^2}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{20}} \approx 0.447$. Since $\zeta < 1$, the original system is underdamped — consistent with the complex pole pair.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the conjugate:** When mapping complex s-plane poles, students often compute only one pole and forget its conjugate. The characteristic equation must have **real coefficients**, so poles must occur in conjugate pairs.
> 2. **Mixing up $\omega$ and $\Omega$:** The angle $\omega T$ in $z = e^{j\omega T}$ is in **radians/sample**, not radians/sec. Converting back to analog frequency requires dividing by $T$.
> 3. **Stability test confusion:** Always check $\vert z \vert < 1$ **strictly** for BIBO stability. If $\vert z \vert = 1$ exactly (on the unit circle), the system is **marginally stable** (oscillatory), not asymptotically stable.
> 4. **Primary strip ignorance:** Students sometimes map poles at frequencies above $\pi/T$ without realizing this causes aliasing. Always confirm $\omega < \pi/T$ before applying the mapping.
> 5. **Radian vs degree trap:** When computing $\cos(\omega T)$ and $\sin(\omega T)$, ensure the calculator is in **radian mode** — a very common arithmetic error costing 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mapping from S-Plane to Z-Plane**
>
> - **Master Equation:** $z = e^{sT}$ (with $T$ = sampling period). Inverse: $s = (1/T)\ln z$.
> - **Magnitude Rule:** $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ — depends **only** on the real part of $s$.
> - **Angle Rule:** $\angle z = \omega T$ — depends **only** on the imaginary part of $s$ (mod $2\pi$).
> - **Stability Equivalence:** LHP ↔ $\vert z \vert < 1$; $j\omega$-axis ↔ $\vert z \vert = 1$; RHP ↔ $\vert z \vert > 1$.
> - **Primary Strip:** $-\pi/T \le \omega \le \pi/T$ — only region that maps bijectively to the z-plane.
> - **Constant $\zeta$:** becomes a logarithmic spiral $r = \exp\!\left(-\zeta\theta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}\right)$.
> - **Constant $\omega_n$:** becomes $(\ln r)^2 + \theta^2 = (\omega_n T)^2$ — circle in log-polar coordinates.
> - **Nyquist Frequency:** $\omega_N = \pi/T$ — half the sampling frequency; frequencies above this cause aliasing.
> - **Discrete pole check:** For conjugate pair $z = a \pm jb$, magnitude is $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$ — must be $<1$ for stability.
> - **Mapping direction:** Always convert $s \to z$ for discretization; for reverse design (digital → analog), use $s = (1/T)\ln z$.
> - **Common KTU trap:** Forgetting that poles at the origin in $z$ ($\vert z \vert = 0$) correspond to $s = -\infty$ in the analog domain.
> - **Exam tip:** Always write the magnitude and angle separately when computing discrete poles — examiners reward clear, structured steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability Analysis of Linear Digital Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Stability Analysis of Linear Digital Control Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Stability in Digital Control Systems In the context of a **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) discrete-time system** characterized by a pulse transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{Y(z)}{U(z)}$, the concept of stability is mathematically formalized through the location of the **closed-loop poles** (the roots of the characteristic equation) in the complex $z$-plane. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A linear digital control system is said to be **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if every bounded input sequence $u(k)$ produces a bounded output sequence $y(k)$ for all $k \geq 0$. Mathematically, this requires that the output $y(k)$ approaches a finite limit as $k \to \infty$ when subjected to a bounded input. The **Closed-Loop Pulse Transfer Function** of a unity feedback digital control system is given by: $$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$ The denominator polynomial $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ is called the **Characteristic Equation (CE)**. The roots of this characteristic equation are the **closed-loop poles**, and their position in the $z$-plane entirely dictates the system's stability. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Boat in a Lake"** > Imagine pushing a small boat across a perfectly still lake (the equilibrium state). Now consider three scenarios for what happens after you stop pushing: > > 1. **Stable System (Asymptotically Stable):** The boat glides forward briefly, then gradually comes to rest at a new position. The disturbances die out over time. → **All poles strictly INSIDE the unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$**. > 2. **Marginally Stable (Critically Stable):** The boat continues to oscillate back and forth at constant amplitude forever, never stopping, but never growing larger. → **Simple poles ON the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$**, with no repeated poles on the circle. > 3. **Unstable System:** The boat's oscillations grow larger and larger until it eventually capsizes or leaves the lake. → **At least one pole OUTSIDE the unit circle $\vert z \vert > 1$**, OR repeated poles on the unit circle. ### 1.3 The Three Categories of Stability (KTU Board Standard) | Stability Type | Pole Location in $z$-plane | Time-Domain Behavior | |---|---|---| | **Asymptotically Stable** | All poles strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all $i$) | Transients decay to zero; $y(k) \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ | | **Marginally Stable** | Simple poles on the unit circle, no poles outside, no repeated unit-circle poles | Sustained constant-amplitude oscillations | | **Unstable** | At least one pole outside the unit circle, or repeated poles on the unit circle | Output grows without bound (explodes) | ### 1.4 Mapping Between the $s$-plane and the $z$-plane The fundamental relationship between continuous and discrete domains is established through the sampling operation: $$z = e^{sT_s}$$ where $T_s$ is the **sampling period** (in seconds). Substituting $s = \sigma + j\omega$: $$z = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T_s} = e^{\sigma T_s} \cdot e^{j\omega T_s}$$ This implies: $$\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T_s}, \quad \angle z = \omega T_s$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Mapping Insight (High-Yield for KTU):** > - The **Left Half of the $s$-plane** ($\sigma < 0$) maps into the **Interior of the Unit Circle** ($\vert z \vert < 1$). > - The **Imaginary Axis of the $s$-plane** ($\sigma = 0$) maps onto the **Unit Circle** ($\vert z \vert = 1$). > - The **Right Half of the $s$-plane** ($\sigma > 0$) maps into the **Exterior of the Unit Circle** ($\vert z \vert > 1$). > > Therefore, the entire infinite left half of the $s$-plane is compressed into the finite unit disc of the $z$-plane through this exponential mapping. **Strips in the $s$-plane map into overlapping sectors in the $z$-plane** because the angle $\omega T_s$ is periodic with period $2\pi$. The primary strip of interest is: $$-\frac{\omega_s}{2} \leq \omega \leq \frac{\omega_s}{2}$$ where $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_s}$ is the **sampling frequency** (in rad/sec). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Conditions for Stability — The Pole Location Theorem For a discrete-time system with characteristic equation $F(z) = 0$ having roots $z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_n$: > [!NOTE] > **Stability Theorem (Pole Location Criterion):** > A digital control system is **stable if and only if all the roots of the characteristic equation $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the $z$-plane, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. The general form of the characteristic polynomial is: $$F(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} z + a_n = 0$$ with $a_0 > 0$ (standard normalization assumed for stability tests). ### 2.2 Method 1 — Jury's Stability Test (Direct $z$-plane Test) Jury's test provides a tabular method to determine whether all roots of a polynomial lie inside the unit circle **without explicitly computing the roots**. It is a direct discrete-time analog of the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **Jury's Stability Conditions** for $F(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n$ with $a_0 > 0$: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Jury Test Table Conditions:** > 1. $F(1) > 0$ > 2. $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ > 3. $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$ > 4. All the elements of the $(n-1)$th row of the Jury array must be positive. ### 2.3 Method 2 — Bilinear Transformation (Routh-Hurwitz via $w$-plane) The bilinear (or Möbius) transformation maps the interior of the unit circle in the $z$-plane onto the **open left half of the $w$-plane**, allowing us to use the familiar continuous-time Routh-Hurwitz criterion. The standard bilinear transformation is: $$z = \frac{1 + w}{1 - w} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad w = \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Geometric Meaning:** The line $\vert z \vert = 1$ (unit circle) maps to the imaginary axis $\text{Re}(w) = 0$ in the $w$-plane. The interior $\vert z \vert < 1$ maps to $\text{Re}(w) < 0$ (left half-plane). The exterior $\vert z \vert > 1$ maps to $\text{Re}(w) > 0$ (right half-plane). After substitution, the resulting polynomial in $w$ is analyzed using the **classical Routh-Hurwitz array**. The system is stable if and only if **all elements of the first column of the Routh array have the same sign** (typically all positive after normalization). ### 2.4 Method 3 — Nyquist Criterion for Discrete Systems The discrete-time Nyquist criterion states: $$Z = N + P$$ where: - $Z$ = number of zeros of $1 + G(z)H(z)$ outside the unit circle - $N$ = number of clockwise encirclements of the point $-1 + j0$ by the Nyquist plot of $G(z)H(z)$ - $P$ = number of poles of $G(z)H(z)$ outside the unit circle > Stability requires $Z = 0$, i.e., $N = -P$. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sampling Relation | $z = e^{sT_s}$ | Maps $s$-plane poles to $z$-plane | | 2 | Stability Region | $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all poles $z_i$ | Direct pole-location check | | 3 | Jury Condition 1 | $F(1) > 0$ | Substitute $z = 1$ | | 4 | Jury Condition 2 | $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ | Substitute $z = -1$ | | 5 | Jury Condition 3 | $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$ | Compare constant and leading coefficients | | 6 | Bilinear Transform | $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ | $w = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ | | 7 | Routh-Hurwitz (after bilinear) | All first-column entries $> 0$ | Apply to transformed polynomial | | 8 | Discrete Nyquist | $Z = N + P$ | $Z=0$ for stability | | 9 | Magnitude Mapping | $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T_s}$ | $s = \sigma + j\omega$ | | 10 | Angle Mapping | $\angle z = \omega T_s$ | Periodic with $2\pi$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Stability analysis of digital control systems is critical in designing flight control systems, robotic manipulators, digital signal processors, automotive ECUs, and industrial process controllers where sampled-data feedback loops must be guaranteed to remain bounded under all operating conditions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the $s$-to-$z$ Mapping (Geometric Proof) Starting from the sampling relation $z = e^{sT_s}$, with $s = \sigma + j\omega$: $$ \begin{aligned} z &= e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T_s} \\ &= e^{\sigma T_s} \cdot e^{j\omega T_s} \\ &= e^{\sigma T_s} \left[ \cos(\omega T_s) + j\sin(\omega T_s) \right] \end{aligned} $$ The **modulus** (magnitude) of $z$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} \vert z \vert &= \sqrt{\left[e^{\sigma T_s}\cos(\omega T_s)\right]^2 + \left[e^{\sigma T_s}\sin(\omega T_s)\right]^2} \\ &= e^{\sigma T_s}\sqrt{\cos^2(\omega T_s) + \sin^2(\omega T_s)} \\ &= e^{\sigma T_s} \end{aligned} $$ The **argument** (angle) of $z$ is: $$\angle z = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin(\omega T_s)}{\cos(\omega T_s)}\right) = \omega T_s$$ **Step-by-step analysis of the stability region:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma < 0 \quad &\Longrightarrow \quad e^{\sigma T_s} < 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert z \vert < 1 \quad \text{(inside unit circle)} \\ \sigma = 0 \quad &\Longrightarrow \quad e^{0} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert z \vert = 1 \quad \text{(on unit circle)} \\ \sigma > 0 \quad &\Longrightarrow \quad e^{\sigma T_s} > 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert z \vert > 1 \quad \text{(outside unit circle)} \end{aligned} $$ This rigorously proves why stability in the $s$-plane (left half) corresponds to poles inside the unit circle in the $z$-plane. ### 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Jury's Test **Problem:** Determine the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is: $$F(z) = z^3 + 0.5z^2 - 0.4z - 0.1 = 0$$ Identify the coefficients: $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = 0.5$, $a_2 = -0.4$, $a_3 = -0.1$. Note that $a_0 > 0$ ✓ (condition satisfied by convention). **Step 1: Check $F(1) > 0$:** $$ \begin{aligned} F(1) &= (1)^3 + 0.5(1)^2 - 0.4(1) - 0.1 \\ &= 1 + 0.5 - 0.4 - 0.1 \\ &= 1.0 \end{aligned} $$ Since $F(1) = 1.0 > 0$ ✓ **Condition 1 is satisfied.** **Step 2: Check $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$** (with $n = 3$): $$ \begin{aligned} F(-1) &= (-1)^3 + 0.5(-1)^2 - 0.4(-1) - 0.1 \\ &= -1 + 0.5 + 0.4 - 0.1 \\ &= -0.2 \\ (-1)^3 F(-1) &= (-1)(-0.2) = 0.2 \end{aligned} $$ Since $0.2 > 0$ ✓ **Condition 2 is satisfied.** **Step 3: Check $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$:** $$\vert a_3 \vert = \vert -0.1 \vert = 0.1, \quad a_0 = 1$$ Since $0.1 < 1$ ✓ **Condition 3 is satisfied.** **Step 4: Construct the Jury Array** and check the final row: | Row | $z^0$ | $z^1$ | $z^2$ | $z^3$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $-0.1$ | $-0.4$ | $0.5$ | $1.0$ | | 2 | $1.0$ | $0.5$ | $-0.4$ | $-0.1$ | The row-1 and row-2 entries are obtained by reversing the original coefficient order. We then compute the $b_i$ and $c_i$ rows: **Row 3 ($b$ row):** $$ \begin{aligned} b_0 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & -0.1 \end{vmatrix} / 1.0 = \frac{(-0.1)(-0.1) - (1.0)(1.0)}{1.0} = 0.01 - 1.0 = -0.99 \\ b_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.1 & 0.5 \\ 1.0 & 0.5 \end{vmatrix} / 1.0 = \frac{(-0.1)(0.5) - (1.0)(0.5)}{1.0} = -0.05 - 0.5 = -0.55 \\ b_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.1 & -0.4 \\ 1.0 & -0.4 \end{vmatrix} / 1.0 = \frac{(-0.1)(-0.4) - (1.0)(-0.4)}{1.0} = 0.04 + 0.4 = 0.44 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Check positivity of $(n-1)$th row entries:** The $b$-row entries are $-0.99$, $-0.55$, $0.44$ — **NOT all positive.** > [!WARNING] > **Result:** Condition 4 is **NOT satisfied** because the $b_0$ and $b_1$ entries are negative. Therefore, **the system is UNSTABLE** (at least one root of the characteristic equation lies outside the unit circle). ### 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Bilinear Transformation Method **Problem:** Investigate the stability of the closed-loop system with characteristic equation: $$F(z) = z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5 = 0$$ **Step 1: Apply the bilinear transformation** $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$: $$ \begin{aligned} F(z) &= z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5 \\ &= \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 - 1.5\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Multiply throughout by $(1-w)^2$ to clear the denominator:** $$ \begin{aligned} (1+w)^2 - 1.5(1+w)(1-w) + 0.5(1-w)^2 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Expand each term individually:** $$ \begin{aligned} (1+w)^2 &= 1 + 2w + w^2 \\ (1+w)(1-w) &= 1 - w^2 \\ (1-w)^2 &= 1 - 2w + w^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Substitute and combine:** $$ \begin{aligned} (1 + 2w + w^2) - 1.5(1 - w^2) + 0.5(1 - 2w + w^2) &= 0 \\ 1 + 2w + w^2 - 1.5 + 1.5w^2 + 0.5 - w + 0.5w^2 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Collect like terms in $w$:** $$ \begin{aligned} w^2 \text{ terms: } & 1 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 3.0 \\ w^1 \text{ terms: } & 2 - 1 = 1.0 \\ w^0 \text{ terms: } & 1 - 1.5 + 0.5 = 0 \\ \end{aligned} $$ Therefore the transformed polynomial is: $$3w^2 + 1w + 0 = w(3w + 1) = 0$$ **Step 6: Apply Routh-Hurwitz:** The roots in $w$ are $w_1 = 0$ and $w_2 = -1/3$. $$ \begin{aligned} w_1 = 0 \quad &\text{lies ON the imaginary axis (marginally stable component)} \\ w_2 = -1/3 \quad &\text{lies in the LEFT half of the } w\text{-plane (stable)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The presence of a root on the imaginary axis of the $w$-plane (which corresponds to a root on the unit circle in the $z$-plane) makes the system **marginally stable** (neither asymptotically stable nor unstable). This is a typical KTU board answer format. ### 3.4 Stability Range Determination (Gain-Dependent Analysis) **Problem:** Find the range of $K$ for which the system with characteristic equation $F(z) = z^2 + (K - 1.5)z + 0.5K = 0$ is stable. **Step 1: Apply Jury's necessary conditions:** *Condition 1:* $F(1) > 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 + (K - 1.5) + 0.5K &> 0 \\ 1 + K - 1.5 + 0.5K &> 0 \\ 1.5K - 0.5 &> 0 \\ 1.5K &> 0.5 \\ K &> \frac{1}{3} \end{aligned} $$ *Condition 2:* $(-1)^2 F(-1) > 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} F(-1) &= 1 - (K - 1.5) + 0.5K \\ &= 1 - K + 1.5 + 0.5K \\ &= 2.5 - 0.5K > 0 \\ K &< 5 \end{aligned} $$ *Condition 3:* $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0$: $$\vert 0.5K \vert < 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -2 < K < 2$$ **Step 2: Combine all conditions:** $$\frac{1}{3} < K < 2$$ **Step 3: Apply Schur-Cohn / final-row condition for $n = 2$:** For a second-order polynomial $a_0 z^2 + a_1 z + a_2$, the additional condition is: $$a_0 + a_1 + a_2 > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad a_0 - a_2 > 0$$ The condition $a_0 - a_2 > 0$ gives $1 - 0.5K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 2$, which is already satisfied. **Step 4: Final stability range:** $\boxed{\frac{1}{3} < K < 2}$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mapping Between $s$-plane and $z$-plane (Stability Regions) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph sPlane["s-PLANE (Continuous Domain)"] sA["Right Half s-plane
sigma greater than 0
UNSTABLE region"] sB["Imaginary Axis
sigma equals 0
j-omega axis
MARGINALLY STABLE"] sC["Left Half s-plane
sigma less than 0
STABLE region"] end subgraph zPlane["z-PLANE (Discrete Domain)"] zA["Exterior of Unit Circle
mod z greater than 1
UNSTABLE region"] zB["Unit Circle
mod z equals 1
MARGINALLY STABLE"] zC["Interior of Unit Circle
mod z less than 1
STABLE region"] end sA -. "z = exp to power sTs" .-> zA sB -. "z = exp to power sTs" .-> zB sC -. "z = exp to power sTs" .-> zC style sA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style sB fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cccc00,color:#000 style sC fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#00cc00,color:#000 style zA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style zB fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cccc00,color:#000 style zC fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#00cc00,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stability Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Closed-Loop
Digital System
with Gain K"] --> B["Extract Characteristic
Equation Fz = 0"] B --> C{"Which Method
to Apply?"} C --> D["METHOD 1:
Direct Pole
Computation"] C --> E["METHOD 2:
Jury Stability
Test"] C --> F["METHOD 3:
Bilinear Transform
to w-plane"] C --> G["METHOD 4:
Discrete Nyquist
Criterion"] D --> H["Check mod zi
less than 1"] E --> I["Verify Jury
Conditions 1-4"] F --> J["Apply Routh-Hurwitz
on w-polynomial"] G --> K["Plot GH and Count
Encirclements of -1"] H --> L{"All Conditions
Satisfied?"} I --> L J --> L K --> L L --> M["STABLE SYSTEM
BIBO Stable"] L --> N["UNSTABLE SYSTEM
At least one pole outside"] L --> O["MARGINALLY STABLE
Poles on unit circle"] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style L fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style M fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#00cc00,color:#000 style N fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style O fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cccc00,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Pole Position vs. System Response ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Stable: All poles mod zi less than 1 [*] --> Marginal: Simple poles on unit circle [*] --> Unstable: Any pole mod zi greater than 1 Stable --> Marginal: Pole crosses unit circle Marginal --> Unstable: Pole moves outside Marginal --> Stable: Pole moves inside Unstable --> Marginal: Pole returns to circle note right of Stable: Transients decay exponentially note right of Marginal: Sustained oscillations note right of Unstable: Output grows unbounded ``` ### 4.4 Jury Test Tabular Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Start: Given Fz of order n"] --> B["Normalize so a0 greater than 0"] B --> C["Check F of 1 greater than 0"] C --> D{"F1 greater than 0?"} D -->|No| X1["UNSTABLE - Stop"] D -->|Yes| E["Check -1 to power n F of -1 greater than 0"] E --> F{"Passes?"} F -->|No| X1 F -->|Yes| G["Check mod an less than a0"] G --> H{"Passes?"} H -->|No| X1 H -->|Yes| I["Construct Jury Array
Compute all rows"] I --> J{"All elements of
last row positive?"} J -->|No| X1 J -->|Yes| K["SYSTEM IS STABLE"] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style K fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#00cc00,color:#000 style X1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the condition for stability of a linear discrete-time system in terms of pole locations in the $z$-plane. How does the imaginary axis of the $s$-plane map into the $z$-plane?** **Model Answer:** The stability condition for a linear discrete-time system in the $z$-plane is: > All poles of the closed-loop pulse transfer function must lie **strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. **Mapping:** The imaginary axis of the $s$-plane (defined by $s = j\omega$, i.e., $\sigma = 0$) maps onto the unit circle of the $z$-plane, since: $$\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T_s} = e^{0 \cdot T_s} = 1$$ The left half of the $s$-plane ($\sigma < 0$) maps to the interior of the unit circle, and the right half ($\sigma > 0$) maps to the exterior. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define BIBO stability. What is the relationship between the $s$-plane stability region and the $z$-plane stability region?** **Model Answer:** **BIBO Stability (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output):** A discrete-time system is BIBO stable if for every bounded input $u(k)$ (i.e., $\vert u(k) \vert \leq M_u < \infty$ for all $k$), the corresponding output $y(k)$ is also bounded (i.e., $\vert y(k) \vert \leq M_y < \infty$ for all $k$). **Relationship:** Under the mapping $z = e^{sT_s}$: - The **entire left half of the $s$-plane** ($\sigma < 0$) is mapped into the **interior of the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert < 1$). - The **right half of the $s$-plane** ($\sigma > 0$) is mapped into the **exterior of the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert > 1$). - The **imaginary axis** ($\sigma = 0$) maps onto the **unit circle** itself. Thus, the $z$-plane stability region (interior of the unit circle) is the "compressed image" of the $s$-plane left half-plane. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Determine the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is given by $F(z) = z^3 - 1.1z^2 + 0.3z + 0.2 = 0$ using **Jury's stability test**. (7 marks) **(b)** Find the range of values of the gain $K$ for which the closed-loop system with characteristic equation $F(z) = z^2 + (K - 1)z + 0.4K = 0$ is stable using **Jury's test**. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) Solution:** Given: $F(z) = z^3 - 1.1z^2 + 0.3z + 0.2 = 0$ Coefficients: $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = -1.1$, $a_2 = 0.3$, $a_3 = 0.2$ (with $a_0 > 0$ ✓) **Condition 1: $F(1) > 0$** $$ \begin{aligned} F(1) &= 1 - 1.1 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.4 \end{aligned} $$ Since $0.4 > 0$ ✓ **Condition 2: $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ with $n = 3$** $$ \begin{aligned} F(-1) &= -1 - 1.1 - 0.3 + 0.2 = -2.2 \\ (-1)^3 F(-1) &= (-1)(-2.2) = 2.2 \end{aligned} $$ Since $2.2 > 0$ ✓ **Condition 3: $\vert a_3 \vert < a_0$** $\vert 0.2 \vert = 0.2 < 1$ ✓ **Condition 4: Jury Array Construction** **Row 1 (coefficients in reverse):** $-0.2$, $-0.3$, $1.1$, $-1.0$ **Row 2 (original coefficients):** $-1.0$, $1.1$, $-0.3$, $-0.2$ **Row 3 ($b$ row) — using the determinant formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} b_0 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.2 & -1.0 \\ -1.0 & -0.2 \end{vmatrix} \Big/ (-1.0) = \frac{0.04 - 1.0}{-1.0} = \frac{-0.96}{-1.0} = 0.96 \\ b_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.2 & -0.3 \\ -1.0 & 1.1 \end{vmatrix} \Big/ (-1.0) = \frac{-0.22 - 0.3}{-1.0} = \frac{-0.52}{-1.0} = 0.52 \\ b_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.2 & 0.3 \\ -1.0 & -0.3 \end{vmatrix} \Big/ (-1.0) = \frac{0.06 - (-0.3)}{-1.0} = \frac{0.36}{-1.0} = -0.36 \end{aligned} $$ **Row 3 entries:** $0.96$, $0.52$, $-0.36$ The element $b_2 = -0.36$ is **negative**. Therefore **Condition 4 is NOT satisfied.** > **Conclusion:** The system is **UNSTABLE**, as at least one root of the characteristic equation lies outside the unit circle. --- **Part (b) Solution:** Given: $F(z) = z^2 + (K - 1)z + 0.4K = 0$ Coefficients: $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = (K - 1)$, $a_2 = 0.4K$ **Condition 1: $F(1) > 0$** $$ \begin{aligned} 1 + (K - 1) + 0.4K &> 0 \\ 0.4K &> 0 \\ K &> 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Condition 2: $(-1)^2 F(-1) > 0$** $$ \begin{aligned} 1 - (K - 1) + 0.4K &> 0 \\ 1 - K + 1 + 0.4K &> 0 \\ 2 - 0.6K &> 0 \\ K &< \frac{2}{0.6} = \frac{10}{3} \end{aligned} $$ **Condition 3: $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0$** $$ \begin{aligned} \vert 0.4K \vert &< 1 \\ -2.5 < K &< 2.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Combining all three conditions:** $$ \begin{aligned} K &> 0 \\ K &< 10/3 \\ -2.5 < K &< 2.5 \end{aligned} $$ The intersection gives: $$\boxed{0 < K < 2.5}$$ > **The closed-loop digital system is asymptotically stable for $0 < K < 2.5$.** --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **bilinear transformation method** of stability analysis in digital control systems. Apply it to determine the stability of the system with characteristic equation $F(z) = z^2 - z + 0.25 = 0$. (7 marks) **(b)** A unity feedback digital control system has open-loop pulse transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{0.5K}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)}$. Using the **bilateral transformation and Routh-Hurwitz criterion**, determine the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) Solution:** **Bilinear Transformation Method Explanation:** The bilinear (Möbius) transformation $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ is a one-to-one conformal mapping that transforms the interior of the unit circle in the $z$-plane into the entire open left half of the $w$-plane. This allows the well-known continuous-time **Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion** to be applied to discrete-time systems. After substituting $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ in the characteristic polynomial $F(z) = 0$, we obtain a polynomial $F'(w) = 0$. The system is stable if and only if all roots of $F'(w)$ lie in the **left half of the $w$-plane**, which is determined by constructing the Routh array and verifying that the **first column has no sign changes**. **Application to $F(z) = z^2 - z + 0.25 = 0$:** Substituting $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$: $$ \begin{aligned} F(z) &= \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.25 = 0 \end{aligned} $$ Multiply throughout by $(1-w)^2$: $$ \begin{aligned} (1+w)^2 - (1+w)(1-w) + 0.25(1-w)^2 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Expanding each term: $$ \begin{aligned} (1+w)^2 &= 1 + 2w + w^2 \\ (1+w)(1-w) &= 1 - w^2 \\ (1-w)^2 &= 1 - 2w + w^2 \end{aligned} $$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} (1 + 2w + w^2) - (1 - w^2) + 0.25(1 - 2w + w^2) &= 0 \\ 1 + 2w + w^2 - 1 + w^2 + 0.25 - 0.5w + 0.25w^2 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Collecting like terms: $$ \begin{aligned} w^2 \text{ coefficient: } & 1 + 1 + 0.25 = 2.25 \\ w^1 \text{ coefficient: } & 2 - 0.5 = 1.5 \\ w^0 \text{ coefficient: } & 1 - 1 + 0.25 = 0.25 \end{aligned} $$ Resulting polynomial in $w$: $$2.25w^2 + 1.5w + 0.25 = 0$$ **Routh Array:** | $w^2$ | $2.25$ | $0.25$ | |---|---|---| | $w^1$ | $1.5$ | $0$ | | $w^0$ | $0.25$ | | First column: $2.25$, $1.5$, $0.25$ — **all positive** ✓ **Roots in $w$-plane:** $$w = \frac{-1.5 \pm \sqrt{1.5^2 - 4(2.25)(0.25)}}{2(2.25)} = \frac{-1.5 \pm \sqrt{2.25 - 2.25}}{4.5} = \frac{-1.5}{4.5} = -\frac{1}{3}$$ Both roots are at $w_1 = w_2 = -1/3$ (repeated root in the left half-plane). > **Conclusion:** The system is **asymptotically stable** (marginally stable in the strict sense, but with repeated roots in the left half-plane, it remains stable in the BIBO sense for this second-order case). --- **Part (b) Solution:** Given: $G(z) = \dfrac{0.5K}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$ The closed-loop characteristic equation is $1 + G(z) = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 + \frac{0.5K}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} &= 0 \\ (z-1)(z-0.5) + 0.5K &= 0 \\ z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5 + 0.5K &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Substitute $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 - 1.5\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + (0.5 + 0.5K) &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Multiply by $(1-w)^2$: $$ \begin{aligned} (1+w)^2 - 1.5(1+w)(1-w) + (0.5+0.5K)(1-w)^2 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Expanding: $$ \begin{aligned} (1 + 2w + w^2) - 1.5(1 - w^2) + (0.5+0.5K)(1 - 2w + w^2) &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Combining: $$ \begin{aligned} w^2 \text{ coeff: } & 1 + 1.5 + (0.5+0.5K) = 3 + 0.5K \\ w^1 \text{ coeff: } & 2 - (0.5+0.5K) \cdot 2 = 2 - 1 - K = 1 - K \\ w^0 \text{ coeff: } & 1 - 1.5 + (0.5+0.5K) = 0.5K \end{aligned} $$ Transformed polynomial: $$(3 + 0.5K)w^2 + (1 - K)w + 0.5K = 0$$ **Routh Array:** | $w^2$ | $3 + 0.5K$ | $0.5K$ | |---|---|---| | $w^1$ | $1 - K$ | $0$ | | $w^0$ | $0.5K$ | | **Stability conditions (all first-column entries positive):** $$ \begin{aligned} 3 + 0.5K &> 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K > -6 \\ 1 - K &> 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K < 1 \\ 0.5K &> 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K > 0 \end{aligned} $$ > **Range of $K$ for stability: $0 < K < 1$** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting normalization of $a_0 > 0$:** If the leading coefficient is negative, students must multiply the polynomial by $-1$ first. Skipping this will produce wrong Jury results. > 2. **Jury Condition 2 sign error:** Students often forget the $(-1)^n$ factor when evaluating $F(-1)$. For odd $n$, this flips the sign — a critical error. > 3. **Bilinear transformation algebra mistakes:** Forgetting to multiply by $(1-w)^2$ (or the appropriate power) after substitution is a very common error. Always clear denominators **before** combining terms. > 4. **Confusing Jury with Routh-Hurwitz:** Jury's test is applied in the $z$-plane; Routh-Hurwitz is applied in the $w$-plane after bilinear transformation. Applying Routh-Hurwitz directly to a $z$-polynomial is a fundamental conceptual error. > 5. **Ignoring the sampling time $T_s$:** When mapping $s$-plane poles to $z$-plane, $T_s$ is non-zero. Substituting $z = e^{sT_s}$ requires explicit numerical evaluation of the exponential. > 6. **Routh array sign-change misinterpretation:** Sign changes in the first column of the Routh array indicate the **number of right-half-plane roots in $w$**, which corresponds to the number of poles **outside the unit circle in $z$**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stability Analysis of Digital Control Systems** - **BIBO Stability:** All closed-loop poles must satisfy $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ (strictly inside the unit circle). - **Mapping Rule:** $z = e^{sT_s}$. LHS of $s$-plane $\rightarrow$ Inside unit circle. Imaginary axis $\rightarrow$ Unit circle. RHS of $s$-plane $\rightarrow$ Outside unit circle. - **Pole Location Theorem:** Stability $\iff$ All characteristic equation roots are inside the unit circle. - **Three Stability Categories:** - *Asymptotically Stable:* All poles strictly inside. - *Marginally Stable:* Simple poles on unit circle, none outside, none repeated. - *Unstable:* Any pole outside, or repeated poles on the unit circle. - **Jury's Test Conditions (4 conditions):** 1. $F(1) > 0$ 2. $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ 3. $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$ 4. Last row of Jury array must be all positive. - **Bilinear Transformation:** $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ maps unit disc interior to LHP of $w$-plane. Apply classical Routh-Hurwitz after substitution. - **Discrete Nyquist:** $Z = N + P$. Stability requires $Z = 0$. - **Stability Range Problems:** Use Jury's test on a $K$-dependent characteristic equation; the intersection of all Jury inequalities gives the allowable $K$ range. - **Common Pitfall:** Never apply Routh-Hurwitz directly on a $z$-polynomial. Always perform the bilinear transformation first. - **High-Yield Formula:** $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T_s}$ — the modulus of any $z$-plane pole is the exponential of the real part of the corresponding $s$-plane pole scaled by the sampling period. - **Quick Stability Check:** Compute the poles directly (for low-order systems) and verify $\vert z_i \vert < 1$.

topics.title: Routh Hurwitz Criteria, Jury's Test, Root Loci of Digital Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routh Hurwitz Criteria, Jury's Test, Root Loci of Digital Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Routh-Hurwitz Criterion for Discrete-Time Systems
The **Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** is a classical algebraic stability test that determines whether all roots of a real-coefficient polynomial lie strictly in the **left-half of the s-plane (or w-plane)**. For discrete-time (z-domain) systems, the criterion cannot be applied directly because the stability boundary is the **unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$, not the imaginary axis. To bridge this gap, the **Bilinear (W-Plane) Transformation** is employed to map the interior of the unit circle into the left-half of a new complex plane, called the **w-plane**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Boundary Mapping:**
> - **s-plane:** Stability boundary = Imaginary axis ($\text{Re}(s) = 0$)
> - **z-plane:** Stability boundary = Unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$)
> - **w-plane:** Stability boundary = Imaginary axis ($\text{Re}(w) = 0$)
>
> The mapping between z and w is given by:
> $$z = \frac{1+w}{1-w} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad w = \frac{z-1}{z+1}$$

### 1.2 Jury's Stability Test
**Jury's Test** is a direct algebraic stability test performed in the **z-domain** without requiring any frequency transformation. It is specifically designed for polynomials whose stability region is the **interior of the unit circle**, making it the most natural and widely-used stability test for discrete-time control systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a 2D dartboard where the bullseye is the origin and the outer ring is the unit circle. A *stable* digital system is one where *all* "darts" (poles) land strictly inside the bullseye's protective boundary. Jury's Test acts like a series of geometric "rings" of conditions that mathematically verify that every single pole stays inside the protective unit circle.

### 1.3 Root Loci of Digital Control Systems
The **Root Locus** is the trajectory traced by the closed-loop poles of a digital system in the **z-plane** as a system parameter (usually the gain $K$) is varied from $0$ to $\infty$. Since stability in the z-plane is determined by the unit circle, the root locus method must be adapted to account for the **non-linear, circular nature** of the stability boundary, rather than the straight-line imaginary axis of the s-plane.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Difference from Continuous Root Locus:** In the s-plane, asymptotes are straight lines radiating from a real-axis breakaway. In the z-plane, the **asymptotes are circles** (or portions of them) converging at angles determined by the difference in the number of poles and zeros.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic for Routh-Hurwitz in Discrete Systems

**Step 1: Obtain the Characteristic Equation**
Derive the closed-loop characteristic equation in the z-domain:
$$F(z) = 1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$

**Step 2: Apply the Bilinear Transformation**
Substitute $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ into $F(z) = 0$, multiply through by $(1-w)^n$ to clear denominators, and obtain $F(w) = 0$.

**Step 3: Verify Real Coefficients**
For the Routh array to be valid, the transformed polynomial $F(w)$ must have **all real coefficients**. This is guaranteed when all coefficients of $F(z)$ are real.

**Step 4: Construct the Routh Array**
Build the standard Routh array using the coefficients of $F(w)$. Apply the auxiliary equation procedure if any row of zeros appears (indicating symmetrical roots on the imaginary axis).

**Step 5: Apply the Routh-Hurwitz Stability Condition**
A necessary and sufficient condition for stability is that **all elements of the first column of the Routh array have the same sign** (no sign changes).

**Step 6: Find the Critical Gain**
The value of $K$ that places a root exactly on the imaginary axis (i.e., on the unit circle of the z-plane) is found by setting the Routh array first-column element to zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Works:** The bilinear transformation $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ is a conformal map that preserves the stability region. The interior of the unit circle in z maps to the open left-half of the w-plane, so all the well-known Routh-Hurwitz theorems apply *unchanged* once the transformation is performed.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Jury's Test

Consider the characteristic polynomial:
$$F(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} z + a_n \quad ; \quad a_0 > 0$$

For all roots to lie strictly **inside the unit circle** (stable system), the following four conditions must be satisfied **simultaneously**:

- **Condition 1:** $F(1) > 0$
- **Condition 2:** $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$
- **Condition 3:** $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$
- **Condition 4:** All the $(n-1)$ determinants $\Delta_k$ of the Jury array must be **positive**, i.e., $\Delta_k > 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$.

The Jury array is constructed by reversing the coefficient sequence of $F(z)$ and then performing successive determinant-based row reductions until a triangular structure is obtained.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic for Root Locus in the Z-Plane

**Step 1: Write the open-loop transfer function** $G(z)H(z) = K \frac{\prod(z - z_i)}{\prod(z - p_i)}$.

**Step 2: Apply the standard rules:**
- Number of branches = number of open-loop poles $n$
- Symmetric about the real axis
- Real-axis segments: To the **left** of an odd number of real poles + zeros
- Asymptote angles: $\theta_k = \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{n-m}$, for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, n-m-1$
- Centroid: $\sigma_a = \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_i}{n - m}$ (in the z-plane, this is the point from which circular asymptotes originate)

**Step 3: Find the intersection of the locus with the unit circle** by solving:
$$1 + G(e^{j\omega})H(e^{j\omega}) = 0$$
This yields the **critical gain** $K_{max}$ for stability.

**Step 4: Breakaway points** satisfy $\frac{dK}{dz} = 0$, where:
$$K = -\frac{\prod(z - p_i)}{\prod(z - z_i)}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Bilinear Transformation | $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ | $w = \frac{z-1}{z+1}$ |
| 2 | Stable Region in z | $\vert z \vert < 1$ | Interior of unit circle |
| 3 | Stable Region in w | $\text{Re}(w) < 0$ | Left half of w-plane |
| 4 | Jury's Condition 1 | $F(1) > 0$ | Substitute $z = 1$ |
| 5 | Jury's Condition 2 | $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ | Substitute $z = -1$ |
| 6 | Jury's Condition 3 | $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$ | Compare constants |
| 7 | Jury's Condition 4 | $\Delta_k > 0$ for $k = 1, \ldots, n-1$ | Jury array determinants |
| 8 | Routh Stability | All 1st column elements have same sign | No sign change |
| 9 | Asymptote Angle (z-plane) | $\theta_k = \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{n-m}$ | $k = 0, 1, \ldots, n-m-1$ |
| 10 | Asymptote Centroid | $\sigma_a = \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_i}{n-m}$ | Real-axis convergence point |
| 11 | Breakaway Equation | $\frac{dK}{dz} = 0$ | Solve for $z$ on real axis |
| 12 | Critical Gain (Unit Circle) | $1 + G(e^{j\omega})H(e^{j\omega}) = 0$ | Solve for $K_{max}$ |
| 13 | Angle Criterion | $\angle G(z)H(z) = \pm 180^\circ (2k+1)$ | Phase condition |
| 14 | Magnitude Criterion | $\vert G(z)H(z) \vert = \frac{1}{K}$ | Magnitude condition |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Digital PID Controllers:** Jury's test is used to tune discrete-time compensators in industrial DCS systems.
- **Sampled-Data Aerospace Systems:** Root locus in z-plane is used to design flight-control gains where the sampling period introduces constraints.
- **Audio/Speech Processing DSP:** Routh-Hurwitz on the w-plane helps design stable IIR digital filters.
- **Robotics & CNC Machines:** Discrete root locus guides real-time gain selection for sampled joint-position control loops.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bilinear Transformation

Starting with the forward difference approximation for integration:
$$s = \frac{1-z^{-1}}{T} = \frac{z-1}{Tz}$$

We need a transformation that maps the **left-half s-plane** into the **interior of the unit circle**. Substituting $s = w$ as a new variable and equating:
$$w = \frac{z-1}{z+1}$$

Solving for $z$:
$$w(z+1) = z-1 \implies wz + w = z - 1$$
$$wz - z = -1 - w \implies z(w-1) = -(1+w)$$
$$z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$$

**Verification on unit circle:** Let $z = e^{j\omega} = \cos\omega + j\sin\omega$. Then:
$$w = \frac{e^{j\omega} - 1}{e^{j\omega} + 1} = \frac{(e^{j\omega/2})(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2})}{(e^{j\omega/2})(e^{j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega/2})} = \frac{2j\sin(\omega/2)}{2\cos(\omega/2)} = j\tan(\omega/2)$$

Since $w = j\tan(\omega/2)$ is **purely imaginary**, the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ maps exactly to the imaginary axis $\text{Re}(w) = 0$. This proves the transformation is conformal and preserves the stability boundary.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Routh-Hurwitz via Bilinear Transformation

**Problem:** A sampled-data system has the characteristic equation:
$$F(z) = z^3 - 1.3z^2 + 0.08z + 0.24 = 0$$

Determine stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply the bilinear transformation** $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$.

$$\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^3 - 1.3\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 + 0.08\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.24 = 0$$

**Step 2: Multiply through by $(1-w)^3$** to clear denominators.

$$(1+w)^3 - 1.3(1+w)^2(1-w) + 0.08(1+w)(1-w)^2 + 0.24(1-w)^3 = 0$$

**Step 3: Expand each term individually.**

First term:
$$(1+w)^3 = 1 + 3w + 3w^2 + w^3$$

Second term expansion $(1+w)^2(1-w) = (1 + 2w + w^2)(1 - w) = 1 - w + 2w - 2w^2 + w^2 - w^3 = 1 + w - w^2 - w^3$. Multiplying by $-1.3$:
$$-1.3(1 + w - w^2 - w^3) = -1.3 - 1.3w + 1.3w^2 + 1.3w^3$$

Third term expansion $(1+w)(1-w)^2 = (1+w)(1 - 2w + w^2) = 1 - 2w + w^2 + w - 2w^2 + w^3 = 1 - w - w^2 + w^3$. Multiplying by $0.08$:
$$0.08(1 - w - w^2 + w^3) = 0.08 - 0.08w - 0.08w^2 + 0.08w^3$$

Fourth term expansion $(1-w)^3 = 1 - 3w + 3w^2 - w^3$. Multiplying by $0.24$:
$$0.24(1 - 3w + 3w^2 - w^3) = 0.24 - 0.72w + 0.72w^2 - 0.24w^3$$

**Step 4: Sum all expanded terms, grouping by powers of $w$.**

Constant term: $1 - 1.3 + 0.08 + 0.24 = 0.02$

$w^1$ term: $3 - 1.3 - 0.08 - 0.72 = 0.9$

$w^2$ term: $3 + 1.3 - 0.08 + 0.72 = 4.94$

$w^3$ term: $1 + 1.3 + 0.08 - 0.24 = 2.14$

Thus:
$$F(w) = 2.14w^3 + 4.94w^2 + 0.9w + 0.02 = 0$$

**Step 5: Construct the Routh Array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $w^3$ | 2.14 | 0.9 | 0 |
| $w^2$ | 4.94 | 0.02 | 0 |
| $w^1$ | $b_1$ | 0 | |
| $w^0$ | $c_1$ | | |

Calculate $b_1$:
$$b_1 = \frac{(4.94)(0.9) - (2.14)(0.02)}{4.94} = \frac{4.446 - 0.0428}{4.94} = \frac{4.4032}{4.94} \approx 0.8913$$

Calculate $c_1$:
$$c_1 = \frac{(0.8913)(0.02) - (4.94)(0)}{0.8913} = 0.02$$

**Step 6: Check first column** $\{2.14, 4.94, 0.8913, 0.02\}$ — all are **positive** with **no sign changes**.

> **Conclusion:** All roots of $F(w)$ lie in the left-half w-plane, hence all roots of $F(z)$ lie **inside the unit circle**. The system is **STABLE**.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Jury's Stability Test

**Problem:** Test the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is:
$$F(z) = z^3 + 0.5z^2 + 0.4z + 0.1 = 0$$

Here $n = 3$, $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = 0.5$, $a_2 = 0.4$, $a_3 = 0.1$.

**Condition 1: $F(1) > 0$**
$$F(1) = 1 + 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.1 = 2.0 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Condition 2: $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$**
$$F(-1) = (-1)^3 + 0.5(-1)^2 + 0.4(-1) + 0.1 = -1 + 0.5 - 0.4 + 0.1 = -0.8$$
$$(-1)^3 F(-1) = (-1)(-0.8) = 0.8 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Condition 3: $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$**
$$\vert a_3 \vert = 0.1 < a_0 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Condition 4: Construct the Jury Array**

| Row | $z^0$ | $z^1$ | $z^2$ | $z^3$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| 1 | $0.1$ | $0.4$ | $0.5$ | $1$ |
| 2 | $1$ | $0.5$ | $0.4$ | $0.1$ |
| 3 | $\Delta_1$ | $\Delta_2$ | | |
| 4 | $0.8913$ | $0.4913$ | | |
| 5 | $\delta_1$ | | | |
| 6 | $0.497$ | | | |

Wait — let me re-derive this properly using the **standard Jury array procedure** for $n=3$:

For $F(z) = a_0 z^3 + a_1 z^2 + a_2 z + a_3$:

$$\Delta_1 = \begin{vmatrix} a_0 & a_3 \\ a_3 & a_0 \end{vmatrix} = a_0^2 - a_3^2 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\Delta_2 = \begin{vmatrix} a_0 & a_2 \\ a_3 & a_1 \end{vmatrix} = a_0 a_1 - a_2 a_3 = (1)(0.5) - (0.4)(0.1) = 0.46 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

All four conditions are satisfied.

> **Conclusion:** The system is **STABLE**.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Test | Domain | Transformation Required? | Number of Conditions | Output Type |
|------|--------|--------------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| Routh-Hurwitz | w-plane | Yes (Bilinear) | 1 (sign consistency) | Binary stable/unstable + critical $K$ |
| Jury's Test | z-plane (direct) | No | 4 explicit checks | Binary stable/unstable |
| Root Locus | z-plane | No | 11 construction rules | Graphical + critical $K_{max}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stability Analysis Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start: Characteristic Equation Fz equals 0"] --> nodeB["Choose Stability Test Method"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Test Selection Criteria"}
    nodeC --> nodeD["Routh Hurwitz via W Plane"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Jurys Test in Z Plane"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Root Locus in Z Plane"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Apply Bilinear Transformation z equals 1 plus w over 1 minus w"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Construct Routh Array"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"All first column entries same sign"}
    nodeI --> nodeJ["System STABLE"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["System UNSTABLE"]
    nodeE --> nodeL["Check Condition 1 F1 greater than 0"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Check Condition 2 minus 1 to power n F minus 1 greater than 0"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Check Condition 3 magnitude of a sub n less than a sub 0"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Check Condition 4 All Jury determinants positive"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["All conditions satisfied"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["System STABLE"]
    nodeO --> nodeR["Any condition fails"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["System UNSTABLE"]
    nodeF --> nodeT["Plot poles and zeros"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["Find real axis segments and breakaway points"]
    nodeU --> nodeV["Find asymptote angles and centroid"]
    nodeV --> nodeW["Find unit circle intersection for K max"]
    nodeW --> nodeX["Identify stable gain range 0 less than K less than K max"]
```

### 4.2 Bilinear Transformation Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Z Plane Unit Circle Stability Region"] -->|"z equals 1 plus w over 1 minus w"| nodeB["W Plane Left Half Plane"]
    nodeB -->|"w equals z minus 1 over z plus 1"| nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeC["Interior of Unit Circle Stable"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Left Half of W Plane Stable"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Conformal One to One Mapping"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
```

### 4.3 Jury's Test Procedural Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Input Fz polynomial"] --> nodeB["Step 1 Evaluate F at z equals 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2 Evaluate F at z equals minus 1 with sign factor"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3 Compare magnitude of a sub n with a sub 0"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 4 Build Jury Array"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute all delta k determinants"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"All four conditions positive"}
    nodeG --> nodeH["Output System STABLE"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Output System UNSTABLE with specific failure reason"]
```

### 4.4 Root Locus Construction in Z-Plane Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Input Gz Hz open loop transfer function"] --> nodeB["Identify number of poles n and zeros m"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Plot poles and zeros in z plane"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Mark real axis segments to left of odd pole zero count"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Compute asymptote angles and centroid"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Find breakaway and break in points dK over dz equals 0"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Find j omega crossing using z equals e to power j omega"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Sketch complete root locus"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Determine stable range of K from unit circle intersection"]
```
