# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define state space representation of a control system. Why is it preferred over transfer function model for modern control applications?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The state space representation of a control system expresses the system dynamics as a set of first-order coupled matrix differential equations of the form $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$, $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$, where $x(t)$ is the **$n \times 1$ state vector**, $u(t)$ is the input, $y(t)$ is the output, and $A, B, C, D$ are constant matrices of appropriate dimensions. *(1 mark)*

It is preferred over the transfer function model for the following reasons *(2 marks)*:
1. It captures **internal dynamics** (states), not just input–output behavior.
2. It handles **MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output)** systems naturally, whereas TF is limited to SISO.
3. It forms the foundation for **modern control techniques** like pole placement, observer design, optimal control, and Kalman filtering.
4. It is amenable to **numerical/symbolic computation** in software like MATLAB and Python.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the Controllable Canonical Form (CCF) of a state space model? Write the general $A$, $B$, and $C$ matrices for a third-order strictly proper transfer function.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Controllable Canonical Form (CCF), also known as the **Phase Variable Form**, is a specific state space realization in which the system matrix $A$ has a companion structure, the input matrix $B$ has a single 1 in the last entry, and the output matrix $C$ contains the numerator coefficients. It is widely used because it simplifies **controllability analysis** — the controllability matrix becomes a lower triangular Toeplitz matrix, which is always full rank for controllable systems. *(1 mark)*

For a third-order strictly proper transfer function

$$G(s) = \frac{b_2 s^2 + b_1 s + b_0}{s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0}$$

the matrices are: *(2 marks)*

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_1 & b_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often **transpose the $C$ matrix** instead of reading off the numerator coefficients in their natural order. Remember: in CCF, $C = [b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{n-1}]$ — *not* transposed.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **sign convention** in the last row of $A$. The coefficients $a_i$ appear with **negative signs**: $-a_0, -a_1, \dots, -a_{n-1}$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **CCF and OCF**. CCF places 1's on the **super-diagonal** (above the main diagonal); OCF places 1's on the **sub-diagonal** (below the main diagonal).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In DCF, forgetting that $C$ contains **residues from PFE**, not the original numerator coefficients.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to verify that the realization has the correct transfer function by computing $C(sI - A)^{-1}B$.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: For the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{2s^2 + 8s + 6}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$:**

**(a)** Obtain the state space model in **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Obtain the state space model in **Observable Canonical Form (OCF)** using duality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — CCF (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify coefficients from the given transfer function.** *(1 mark)*

Comparing $G(s) = \frac{2s^2 + 8s + 6}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$ with the standard form:

$$a_0 = 6, \quad a_1 = 11, \quad a_2 = 6, \quad b_0 = 6, \quad b_1 = 8, \quad b_2 = 2$$

**Step 2: Construct the system matrix $A_{CCF}$ using the companion structure.** *(2 marks)*

$$A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Construct the input matrix $B_{CCF}$.** *(1 mark)*

$$B_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Construct the output matrix $C_{CCF}$ from the numerator coefficients.** *(2 marks)*

$$C_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_1 & b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 8 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Set $D = 0$ since $G(s)$ is strictly proper.** *(1 mark)*

$$\boxed{\{A, B, C, D\}_{CCF} = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 8 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, 0 \right\}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — OCF via Duality (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Recall the duality relationship between CCF and OCF.** *(1 mark)*

$$A_{OCF} = A_{CCF}^T, \quad B_{OCF} = C_{CCF}^T, \quad C_{OCF} = B_{CCF}^T$$

**Step 2: Compute $A_{OCF}$ by transposing $A_{CCF}$.** *(2 marks)*

$$A_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & -6 \\ 1 & 0 & -11 \\ 0 & 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $B_{OCF}$ by transposing $C_{CCF}$.** *(1 mark)*

$$B_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 8 & 2 \end{bmatrix}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute $C_{OCF}$ by transposing $B_{CCF}$.** *(2 marks)*

$$C_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Set $D = 0$.** *(1 mark)*

$$\boxed{\{A, B, C, D\}_{OCF} = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & -6 \\ 1 & 0 & -11 \\ 0 & 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, 0 \right\}}$$

**Verification (Bonus):** Both realizations must yield the same transfer function $G(s)$. Using the formula $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B$, students should verify that the determinant expansion reproduces $G(s) = \frac{2s^2 + 8s + 6}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Consider the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{12}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$.**

**(a)** Obtain the **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)** state space realization using partial fraction expansion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Obtain the **state space model in Phase Variable Form (CCF)** and verify that both realizations yield the same transfer function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — DCF (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify the poles of $G(s)$.** *(1 mark)*

$$p_1 = -1, \quad p_2 = -2, \quad p_3 = -3$$

All three poles are **distinct and real**, so DCF is applicable.

**Step 2: Perform Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE).** *(3 marks)*

$$G(s) = \frac{12}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{r_1}{s+1} + \frac{r_2}{s+2} + \frac{r_3}{s+3}$$

Computing each residue using the cover-up method:

$$r_1 = \left. \frac{12}{(s+2)(s+3)} \right|_{s=-1} = \frac{12}{(1)(2)} = 6$$

$$r_2 = \left. \frac{12}{(s+1)(s+3)} \right|_{s=-2} = \frac{12}{(-1)(1)} = -12$$

$$r_3 = \left. \frac{12}{(s+1)(s+2)} \right|_{s=-3} = \frac{12}{(-2)(-1)} = 6$$

Therefore:

$$G(s) = \frac{6}{s+1} - \frac{12}{s+2} + \frac{6}{s+3}$$

**Step 3: Construct the $A_{DCF}$ matrix (diagonal of poles).** *(1 mark)*

$$A_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Construct $B_{DCF}$ (column of ones).** *(1 mark)*

$$B_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Construct $C_{DCF}$ (row of residues).** *(1 mark)*

$$C_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -12 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

$$\boxed{\{A, B, C, D\}_{DCF} = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -12 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, 0 \right\}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — CCF and Verification (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Expand $G(s)$ into a standard polynomial ratio.** *(2 marks)*

$$G(s) = \frac{12}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{12}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$$

So: $a_0 = 6$, $a_1 = 11$, $a_2 = 6$, $b_0 = 12$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 0$.

**Step 2: Construct the CCF matrices.** *(2 marks)*

$$A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Verify using $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B$.** *(3 marks)*

Compute $(sI - A)$:

$$sI - A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & s & -1 \\ 6 & 11 & s+6 \end{bmatrix}$$

The determinant is:

$$\det(sI - A) = s[s(s+6) + 11] + 1[0 \cdot (s+6) - (-1)(6)] = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6$$

Numerator: $C \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B$ picks the **last column** of the adjugate, which equals the cofactor vector:

$$\text{cofactor}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} s \cdot s - (-1)(-1) \\ 0 \cdot s - (-1)(6) \\ 0 \cdot 11 - s \cdot 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s^2 - 1 \\ 6 \\ -6s \end{bmatrix}$$

So:

$$C \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s^2 - 1 \\ 6 \\ -6s \end{bmatrix} = 12(s^2 - 1)$$

Therefore:

$$G(s) = \frac{12(s^2 - 1)}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$$

Wait — this is incorrect! Let me re-verify. For the original $G(s) = \frac{12}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$, the numerator should be just $12$. The issue is that I incorrectly applied CCF for $b_0 = 12, b_1 = 0, b_2 = 0$. Let me check: $C_{CCF} = [b_0, b_1, b_2] = [12, 0, 0]$.

Recomputing:

$$\text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B = \text{last column of adjugate} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ s \end{bmatrix}$$

Then $C \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ s \end{bmatrix} = 12 \cdot 1 + 0 + 0 = 12$. ✓

So $G(s) = \frac{12}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$, which matches. **Both DCF and CCF realizations are verified.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The earlier calculation showing $12(s^2-1)$ was due to an algebraic slip in the cofactor expansion. The corrected verification demonstrates the importance of *carefully evaluating every term*. KTU examiners will deduct marks for any incorrect intermediate step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** — Read this the morning of your exam.

### 📌 Core Definitions
- **State:** Minimum set of variables that completely describe the system's future behavior given its current state and future inputs.
- **State Vector $x$:** Column vector of all state variables, dimension $n \times 1$.
- **State Space Model:** $\{A, B, C, D\}$ matrices describing $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$.
- **Canonical Form:** A *specific structure* of the $A, B, C, D$ matrices for a given TF.
- **Strictly Proper TF:** Numerator degree $<$ denominator degree. Yields $D = 0$.
- **Proper TF:** Numerator degree $\leq$ denominator degree.
- **Improper TF:** Numerator degree $>$ denominator degree (rare in physical systems).

### 📌 Key Conversion Rules
- **Order of TF = Dimension of state vector = Size of $A$ matrix.**
- **In CCF:** Last row of $A$ = $[-a_0, -a_1, \dots, -a_{n-1}]$.
- **In CCF:** $B$ has a single 1 at the *last* entry.
- **In CCF:** $C = [b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{n-1}]$.
- **OCF is the transpose-dual of CCF:** $A_{OCF} = A_{CCF}^T$, $B_{OCF} = C_{CCF}^T$, $C_{OCF} = B_{CCF}^T$.
- **DCF:** $A$ is diagonal with poles; $C$ contains **residues** from PFE.
- **JCF:** Repeated poles yield Jordan blocks with 1's on the super-diagonal.
- **Cascade Form:** TF factored into first/second-order blocks, multiplied in series.
- **Parallel Form:** TF decomposed via PFE into parallel branches.

### 📌 Verification Formula
- Always verify using $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$.

### 📌 Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
1. ❌ Forgetting the **negative signs** on $a_i$ in the last row of $A$.
2. ❌ Mixing up CCF and OCF structures (super-diagonal vs. sub-diagonal placement of 1's).
3. ❌ Using original numerator coefficients instead of PFE residues in DCF.
4. ❌ Failing to handle **repeated poles** by using JCF instead of DCF.
5. ❌ Assuming state space representation is unique — it is **not**, only $G(s)$ is unique.
6. ❌ Setting $D = 0$ for proper (but not strictly proper) transfer functions.
7. ❌ Confusing "$n$-th order TF" with "n state variables" — they are the same!

### 📌 MATLAB / Python Cross-Verification Code
- **MATLAB:** `sys = tf(num, den); ss(sys)` yields a default realization.
- **Python (control library):** `sys = control.tf2ss(num, den)` or `control.StateSpace(...)`.

### 📌 One-Line Memory Hooks
- **CCF:** "**C**ontrollable has **C**ompany in the last **C**olumn of $B$" — i.e., $B = [0, \dots, 0, 1]^T$.
- **OCF:** "**O**bservable has its **O**wn 1 in $C$" — i.e., $C = [0, \dots, 0, 1]$.
- **DCF:** "**D**iagonal has the **D**enominator poles on the **D**iagonal."
- **JCF:** "**J**ordan has the **J**umper (1's) above the diagonal for repeated roots."

### 📌 Question-Pattern Recognition for KTU 2024
- "Obtain state model in phase variable form" → **CCF**
- "Find state model in observable canonical form" → **OCF**
- "Find diagonal canonical form" → **DCF** (check for distinct poles first!)
- "Obtain state space model" (no form specified) → Default to **CCF**
- "Find Jordan form" → **JCF** (check for repeated poles)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT631 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Digital Control Systems**, the **Characteristic Equation** of a discrete-time (sampled-data) control system is the polynomial equation obtained by equating the denominator of the **closed-loop pulse transfer function** to zero. It encapsulates the intrinsic dynamic behaviour of the system and determines the location of the system's natural modes (characteristic roots) in the complex z-plane.

For a single-loop sampled-data system with forward path pulse transfer function **G(z)** and feedback path pulse transfer function **H(z)**, the closed-loop pulse transfer function is:

$$T(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$

The **Characteristic Equation** is therefore defined as:

$$1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The roots of the characteristic equation are called the **characteristic roots** (or closed-loop poles). For the system to be **stable**, all characteristic roots must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** of the z-plane (i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all roots $z_i$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the characteristic equation as the **"DNA fingerprint"** of a digital control system. Just as a person's DNA determines their physical traits, the characteristic equation uniquely determines how a digital system will respond over time when disturbed.

**Real-World Analogy — The Bouncing Ball in a Stadium:**
- Imagine dropping a ball inside a cylindrical stadium with curved walls.
- The characteristic equation is like the **mathematical fingerprint of the stadium's shape**.
- If the equation says the ball's "mode positions" are inside a small circle (the unit circle), the ball eventually **settles at the bottom** → **Stable System**.
- If the modes lie on the circle's boundary, the ball **bounces forever at constant height** → **Marginally Stable System**.
- If the modes lie outside the circle, the ball **flies out of the stadium** → **Unstable System**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Unit Circle Radius:** **1** (in the z-plane, this is the stability boundary).
- **Sampling Period:** **T** (seconds) — fundamental discrete-time parameter.
- **Critical Damping Ratio Equivalent:** For digital systems, poles closer to the origin correspond to **faster response** with **shorter settling time**.
- **Bilinear Transformation Constant:** **T = 2** is the standard substitution period for w-plane mapping.

> [!NOTE]
> The characteristic equation is the **bridge between frequency-domain analysis (z-plane)** and **time-domain response** of a discrete-time system. Solving it tells you whether your digital controller will keep the plant stable or drive it to oscillations/divergence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Characteristic Equation Matters

The characteristic equation governs **three fundamental properties** of a digital control system:

1. **Stability** — Determined entirely by the location of the roots.
2. **Transient Response** — Pole magnitudes dictate settling time and overshoot.
3. **Natural Modes** — Each root corresponds to one exponential mode $z_i^k$ in the time response.

### 2.1.1 Derivation Logic from Closed-Loop Structure

A sampled-data feedback system has the following structure:
- Reference $R(z)$ → Summing junction → Error $E(z)$ → Digital Plant $G(z)$ → Output $C(z)$.
- Output fed back through $H(z)$ to the summing junction.

The error signal equation:

$$E(z) = R(z) - H(z)C(z)$$

Substituting $C(z) = G(z)E(z)$:

$$E(z) = R(z) - H(z)G(z)E(z)$$

$$E(z)[1 + G(z)H(z)] = R(z)$$

Therefore, the closed-loop output becomes:

$$C(z) = \frac{G(z)R(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$

The denominator polynomial $\mathbf{1 + G(z)H(z)}$ when set to zero yields the **characteristic equation**.

## 2.2 State-Space Formulation of the Characteristic Equation

For a discrete-time state-space representation:

$$x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$$
$$y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$$

The characteristic equation is given by the **eigenvalue equation** of the state transition matrix $\Phi$:

$$\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$$

where $\Phi \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the state transition matrix and $I$ is the identity matrix of the same order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The eigenvalues of $\Phi$ are precisely the characteristic roots. The system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if all eigenvalues satisfy $\vert \lambda_i(\Phi) \vert < 1$.

## 2.3 Stability Conditions in the z-Plane

| Condition on Root $\vert z_i \vert$ | System Behaviour |
|:---:|:---|
| $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ (strictly inside unit circle) | **Asymptotically Stable** |
| $\vert z_i \vert = 1$ (on the unit circle, simple roots) | **Marginally Stable** (sustained oscillation) |
| $\vert z_i \vert > 1$ (outside unit circle) | **Unstable** (response grows unbounded) |
| Repeated roots on unit circle | **Unstable** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ | Closed-loop characteristic equation (z-domain) |
| 2 | $T(z) = \dfrac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$ | Closed-loop pulse transfer function |
| 3 | $\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$ | Characteristic equation from state-space |
| 4 | $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all $i$ | Stability criterion in z-plane |
| 5 | $z = \dfrac{1 + w}{1 - w}$ | Bilinear transformation (z to w) |
| 6 | $w = \dfrac{z - 1}{z + 1}$ | Inverse bilinear transformation (w to z) |
| 7 | $T_s \approx \dfrac{-4}{\ln(\vert z_i \vert_{\max})} \cdot T$ | Approximate settling time formula |
| 8 | $\zeta \approx -\cos(\angle z_i)$ | Equivalent damping ratio from pole angle |
| 9 | $z = e^{sT}$ | Mapping from s-plane to z-plane |
| 10 | $\omega_d = \dfrac{1}{T}\angle z_i$ | Equivalent damped natural frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In industries like aerospace, robotics, and process control, the characteristic equation is the **first checkpoint** during controller design. Engineers use root locus techniques in the z-plane to tune digital controllers (PID, lead-lag) so that all characteristic roots remain inside the unit circle for guaranteed stability.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Digital PID Controllers:** Tuning gains by ensuring closed-loop characteristic roots lie within a desired region of the unit circle.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Discrete-state observers (Kalman filters) require characteristic roots of error dynamics inside the unit circle for accurate state estimation.
- **Power Electronics:** Digital SMPS controllers use characteristic equation analysis to avoid subharmonic oscillations.
- **Networked Control Systems:** Delay-induced characteristic root migration is analysed to design stable communication-aware controllers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Characteristic Equation from a Standard Second-Order Sampled System

Consider a sampled-data system with forward path:

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)}$$

Sampling period $T = 1$ second. The corresponding z-transfer function is:

$$G(z) = \frac{z(1 - e^{-T})}{(z - 1)(z - e^{-T})} = \frac{0.632z}{(z - 1)(z - 0.368)}$$

Assuming unity feedback $H(z) = 1$, the closed-loop pulse transfer function:

$$T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)} = \frac{0.632z}{(z-1)(z-0.368) + 0.632z}$$

**Step 1 — Expand the denominator:**

$$(z-1)(z-0.368) = z^2 - 0.368z - z + 0.368 = z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368$$

**Step 2 — Add the feedback term $0.632z$:**

$$z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368 + 0.632z = z^2 - 0.736z + 0.368$$

**Step 3 — Form the characteristic equation by setting denominator to zero:**

$$z^2 - 0.736z + 0.368 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the characteristic roots using the quadratic formula:**

$$z = \frac{0.736 \pm \sqrt{0.736^2 - 4(0.368)}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the discriminant:**

$$0.736^2 = 0.541696$$
$$4 \times 0.368 = 1.472$$
$$\Delta = 0.541696 - 1.472 = -0.930304$$

**Step 6 — Compute the complex square root:**

$$\sqrt{-0.930304} = j\sqrt{0.930304} \approx j\,0.9646$$

**Step 7 — Final roots:**

$$z_{1,2} = \frac{0.736 \pm j\,0.9646}{2} = 0.368 \pm j\,0.4823$$

**Step 8 — Check magnitudes (stability test):**

$$\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert = \sqrt{0.368^2 + 0.4823^2} = \sqrt{0.1354 + 0.2326} = \sqrt{0.368} \approx 0.6066$$

Since $\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert \approx 0.6066 < 1$, **the system is stable** ✓.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Characteristic Equation from State-Space Representation

Given the discrete state-space model of a DC motor position control with sampling period $T = 0.1$ s:

$$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9516 & 0.0975 \\ -0.951 & 0.8561 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Gamma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00484 \\ 0.0975 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Form the matrix $(zI - \Phi)$:**

$$zI - \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.9516 & -0.0975 \\ 0.951 & z - 0.8561 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the determinant:**

$$\det(zI - \Phi) = (z - 0.9516)(z - 0.8561) - (-0.0975)(0.951)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the first product:**

$$(z - 0.9516)(z - 0.8561) = z^2 - 0.8561z - 0.9516z + 0.9516 \times 0.8561$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the constant product:**

$$0.9516 \times 0.8561 = 0.8147$$

**Step 5 — Combine the linear terms:**

$$-0.8561z - 0.9516z = -1.8077z$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the second product term:**

$$(-0.0975)(0.951) = -0.09272$$

So $-(0.0975 \times 0.951)$ with the sign flipped gives $+0.09272$.

**Step 7 — Form the complete polynomial:**

$$z^2 - 1.8077z + 0.8147 + 0.09272 = z^2 - 1.8077z + 0.9074$$

**Step 8 — The characteristic equation is:**

$$z^2 - 1.8077z + 0.9074 = 0$$

**Step 9 — Solve using the quadratic formula:**

$$z = \frac{1.8077 \pm \sqrt{1.8077^2 - 4(0.9074)}}{2}$$

$$1.8077^2 = 3.2678$$
$$4 \times 0.9074 = 3.6296$$
$$\Delta = 3.2678 - 3.6296 = -0.3618$$

**Step 10 — Final roots:**

$$z_{1,2} = \frac{1.8077 \pm j\sqrt{0.3618}}{2} = 0.9039 \pm j\,0.3007$$

**Step 11 — Stability check:**

$$\vert z_{1,2} \vert = \sqrt{0.9039^2 + 0.3007^2} = \sqrt{0.8170 + 0.0904} = \sqrt{0.9074} \approx 0.9526$$

Since $\vert z_i \vert \approx 0.9526 < 1$, the **system is asymptotically stable** ✓.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bilinear Transformation (z to w) for Routh-Hurwitz

For a discrete system with characteristic equation in z:

$$P(z) = z^2 - 0.736z + 0.368 = 0$$

**Step 1 — Apply the substitution $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$:**

$$\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 - 0.736\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.368 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Multiply throughout by $(1-w)^2$:**

$$(1+w)^2 - 0.736(1+w)(1-w) + 0.368(1-w)^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand each term:**

$$(1+w)^2 = 1 + 2w + w^2$$
$$(1+w)(1-w) = 1 - w^2$$
$$(1-w)^2 = 1 - 2w + w^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and collect:**

$$1 + 2w + w^2 - 0.736(1 - w^2) + 0.368(1 - 2w + w^2) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Expand the constant and $w$ terms:**

$$= 1 + 2w + w^2 - 0.736 + 0.736w^2 + 0.368 - 0.736w + 0.368w^2$$

**Step 6 — Group $w^2$, $w$, and constant terms separately:**

$$w^2 \text{ coefficient: } 1 + 0.736 + 0.368 = 2.104$$
$$w \text{ coefficient: } 2 - 0.736 = 1.264$$
$$\text{Constant: } 1 - 0.736 + 0.368 = 0.632$$

**Step 7 — Final characteristic equation in w-plane:**

$$2.104\,w^2 + 1.264\,w + 0.632 = 0$$

**Step 8 — Apply Routh-Hurwitz check:**

All coefficients are positive ⇒ **System is stable** in the w-plane ⇒ Original discrete system is **stable in z-plane** ✓.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Location of Characteristic Equation in a Sampled Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Reference R of z] --> SJ[ Summing Junction ]
    SJ --> E[Error E of z]
    E --> H1[Zero Order Hold G_h0]
    H1 --> P1[Plant G_p of s]
    P1 --> S1[Sampler Switch]
    S1 --> GZ[Pulse Transfer G of z]
    GZ --> C[Output C of z]
    C --> F1[Feedback H of z]
    F1 --> SJ
    GZ -.Characteristic Root.-> CEQ[ 1 plus GzHz equals 0 ]
    F1 -.Forms Denominator.-> CEQ
    CEQ --> STAB[Stability Check: Mod z less than 1 ]
    STAB --> DEC{Decision}
    DEC -->|All inside| OK[Stable System]
    DEC -->|On boundary| MG[Marginally Stable]
    DEC -->|Outside| US[Unstable System]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Characteristic Equation Analysis

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Plant Model Gs] --> B[Z Transform with ZOH]
    B --> C[Discrete Model Gz]
    D[Sensor Model Hz] --> C
    C --> E[Form Closed Loop Tz]
    E --> F[Extract Denominator Polynomial]
    F --> G[Set Polynomial to Zero]
    G --> H[Characteristic Equation]
    H --> I[Method 1: Direct Root Finding]
    H --> J[Method 2: Bilinear W Transform]
    H --> K[Method 3: Jury Stability Test]
    I --> L[Stability Verdict]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Controller Tuning Action]
```

## 4.3 Stability Decision Flowchart for z-Plane Roots

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Obtain Characteristic Equation Pz equals 0] --> Q[Find All Roots z_i]
    Q --> R[Compute Magnitudes mod z_i]
    R --> S{All magnitudes less than 1?}
    S -->|Yes| T[ Asymptotically Stable ]
    S -->|No, some equals 1| U[Check Multiplicity]
    S -->|No, some greater than 1| V[ Unstable ]
    U --> W{Simple roots on boundary?}
    W -->|Yes| X[ Marginally Stable ]
    W -->|No, repeated| V
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the characteristic equation of a digital control system and state the stability condition based on its roots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The characteristic equation of a digital control system is the polynomial obtained by equating the denominator of the closed-loop pulse transfer function to zero, expressed as:

$$1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$

For **asymptotic stability**, all roots $z_i$ of this equation must lie strictly **inside the unit circle** of the z-plane, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for every $i$.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Write the characteristic equation in the state-space form for a discrete-time system described by $x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The characteristic equation in the state-space form is given by:

$$\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$$

where $z$ is the complex variable, $I$ is the identity matrix of the same order as the state transition matrix $\Phi$, and the roots of this determinant equation are the eigenvalues of $\Phi$ (characteristic roots).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question A — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

For a sampled-data control system with forward path pulse transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368}$$

and unity feedback $H(z) = 1$, with sampling period $T = 1$ s:

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop characteristic equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the characteristic roots and comment on the stability of the system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the Characteristic Equation**

The closed-loop pulse transfer function is:

$$T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)} = \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368 + 0.368z + 0.264}$$

Combining the denominator terms:

$$z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368 + 0.368z + 0.264 = z^2 - 1.0z + 0.632$$

The characteristic equation is:

$$z^2 - z + 0.632 = 0$$

**(b) Finding the Characteristic Roots**

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1^2 - 4(0.632)}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 2.528}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{-1.528}}{2}$$

$$z = \frac{1 \pm j\,1.2361}{2} = 0.5 \pm j\,0.618$$

Stability check — magnitude of each root:

$$\vert z_{1,2} \vert = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.618^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.3819} = \sqrt{0.6319} \approx 0.795$$

Since $\vert z_i \vert \approx 0.795 < 1$, **both characteristic roots lie inside the unit circle**, and the system is **asymptotically stable** ✓.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]`

A discrete-time system is described by the state-space matrices:

$$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.1 \\ -0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Gamma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** Derive the characteristic equation in the z-domain using the state-space formulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the bilinear transformation $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ to obtain the w-plane characteristic polynomial and verify stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Characteristic Equation from State-Space**

Form the matrix $(zI - \Phi)$:

$$zI - \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.8 & -0.1 \\ 0.5 & z - 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the determinant:

$$\det(zI - \Phi) = (z - 0.8)(z - 0.6) - (-0.1)(0.5)$$

Expanding:

$$= z^2 - 0.6z - 0.8z + 0.48 + 0.05 = z^2 - 1.4z + 0.53$$

The characteristic equation is:

$$z^2 - 1.4z + 0.53 = 0$$

**(b) Bilinear Transformation and Routh-Hurwitz Verification**

Substituting $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$:

$$\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 - 1.4\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.53 = 0$$

Multiplying by $(1-w)^2$:

$$(1+w)^2 - 1.4(1+w)(1-w) + 0.53(1-w)^2 = 0$$

Expanding each term:

$$1 + 2w + w^2 - 1.4(1 - w^2) + 0.53(1 - 2w + w^2) = 0$$

$$1 + 2w + w^2 - 1.4 + 1.4w^2 + 0.53 - 1.06w + 0.53w^2 = 0$$

Grouping by powers of $w$:

$$w^2: \quad 1 + 1.4 + 0.53 = 2.93$$
$$w^1: \quad 2 - 1.06 = 0.94$$
$$w^0: \quad 1 - 1.4 + 0.53 = 0.13$$

The w-plane polynomial is:

$$2.93\,w^2 + 0.94\,w + 0.13 = 0$$

**Routh-Hurwitz Check:** All coefficients $(2.93, 0.94, 0.13)$ are strictly positive, and the polynomial is of order 2. Therefore, **all w-plane roots lie in the left half-plane** ⇒ the original discrete system is **stable** ✓.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Characteristic Equation Problems:**
> 1. **Sign Error in State-Space Determinant:** Students often forget the negative sign when forming $(zI - \Phi)$. The correct form is $\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$, **not** $\det(\Phi - zI) = 0$. A sign flip will change all root magnitudes and stability verdict — **deduct 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusion Between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation:** The characteristic equation is for the **closed-loop** system, i.e., $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$. Using just the denominator of $G(z)$ is a frequent mistake — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Unit Circle Stability Criterion:** Students sometimes equate z-plane stability to s-plane conditions (Re < 0). Always state explicitly $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for discrete systems — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 4. **Bilinear Transformation Algebra Errors:** Sign errors when applying $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ are very common. Verify by substituting $w=0$ and checking $z=1$ — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 5. **Not Commenting on Stability:** KTU examiners expect a clear **verdict line** ("system is stable / unstable / marginally stable") with justification based on root magnitudes. A bare numerical answer loses **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Characteristic equation = $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ for closed-loop discrete systems.
- **State-Space Form:** $\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$; roots = eigenvalues of $\Phi$.
- **Stability Criterion:** All roots must satisfy $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ (inside unit circle).
- **Boundary Cases:** Simple roots on unit circle → marginally stable; repeated roots on unit circle → unstable.
- **Bilinear Transformation:** Use $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ to map the unit circle interior to the **left half of the w-plane**, enabling Routh-Hurwitz application.
- **Three Solution Methods:**
  1. Direct root finding (factoring / quadratic formula).
  2. Bilinear transformation + Routh-Hurwitz.
  3. **Jury's stability test** (tableau method, no need to find roots).
- **Jury's Test (Quick Recall):** Necessary conditions for stability of $P(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n$:
  1. $P(1) > 0$
  2. $(-1)^n P(-1) > 0$
  3. $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$
- **Settling Time Relation:** $T_s \approx \dfrac{-4T}{\ln(\vert z_{\text{dom}} \vert)}$ where $z_{\text{dom}}$ is the dominant pole.
- **Damping Estimate:** $\zeta \approx -\cos(\theta)$ where $\theta = \angle z_i$.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Place dominant closed-loop poles at $\vert z \vert \approx 0.3 - 0.7$ for **fast yet well-damped** digital response.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always derive the characteristic equation **before** commenting on stability — this is the marker for the bulk of the marks.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** The characteristic equation is the **denominator of $T(z)$** set to zero, not the numerator. Numerator roots are **zeros**, not characteristic roots.
